title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Release Notes Learn about what's new in the VMware Cloud Foundation™ (VCF) 9.x releases, the software components and versions included in the Bill of Materials (BOM), product support notes, and known issues. In This Chapter • VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 Release Notes • Async Releases VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 Release Notes Learn about what's new in the VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) release, the software components and versions included in the Bill of Materials (BOM), product support notes, known and resolved issues. VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 | 12 MAY 2026 | Build 25377994 Check for additions and updates to these release notes. Internationalization VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 is available in the following languages: • English • Japanese • Spanish • French Components of VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 do not accept non-ASCII input. This document contains the following sections: • What's New • Bill of Materials • Product Support Notes • Known Issues • Resolved Issues What's New Following are the key features and capabilities of VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1: • Enhanced NVMe Memory Tiering. Hypervisor tiers active pages to DRAM and cold pages to local NVMe, expanding effective memory cap

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • VKS and VM Fast-Deploy. Significantly faster deployment and upgrading of VKS and VM clusters using linked clone technology, significantly improving operational agility and time to value. • Simplified Container-as-a-Service. Self-service namespace provisioning with registry, ingress, quotas, and identity inherited from VCF constructs; simpler, faster way to provision a few containers without full K8s management. • Native Object Storage (Tech Preview). Self-service S3 object storage for developers, with IT keeping the guardrail; deploy, scale, and manage object storage with the same workflows as block and file. • Live Patching for ESX for TPM-enabled hosts. Patches applied to running kernel memory; VMs continue running with no maintenance window, covering up to 80% of patches. • Continuous Compliance Enforcement. Advanced Cyber Compliance now supports continuous compliance remediation and unified security posture management for the VCF stack. • On-prem Ransomware Recovery. Integrated cyber recovery to on-premises VCF isolated clean rooms. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Compatibility Guest Operating System Compatibility for ESX To determine which guest operating systems are compatible with ESX 9.1, see the Broadcom Compatibility Guide. The following new major guest operating system releases are supported in this release: • Ubuntu 26.04 LTS • SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16 • Debian 13.0 • FreeBSD 15.0 • Pardus 25.0 • CentOS Stream 10 (supported as Tech Preview) • CentOS Stream 9 (supported as Tech Preview) Support for the following guest operating system releases is deprecated or terminated in this release: • RHEL 4.x: Terminated • Oracle Linux 5.x: Terminated • CentOS 4.x and 5.x: Terminated • SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 10 SP4: Terminated • SUSE Linux Enterprise Desktop 12: Terminated • Debian 7.x: Terminated • Flatcar Container Linux 3033 LTS: Terminated • Asianux 3.x: Terminated • Asianux 4.x: Deprecated Windows Guest OS Pending Reboot Reporting with VMware Tools 13.1.0: With VCF 9.1, VMware Tools 13.1.0 adds a vCenter UI and API restart status indicator that allows you to monitor and identify virtual machines with pending restarts after a VMware Tools upgrade. This status allows you to efficiently schedule the pending restarts. For more info

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: API Guest Customization APIs: VCF 9.1 introduces updates to the Guest Customization APIs, focusing on network configuration flexibility and improved automation: • For Powered-Off VMs: – IPv6-Only Support: You can explicitly deacivate IPv4 to configure IPv6-only networking in guest customization by both the UI and API. This capability avoids the need to maintain a parallel IPv4 configuration. – Partial Network Customization: You can customize network settings by providing only network-related fields to Guest Customization API. This capability streamlines operations by avoiding the need to supply the full customization profile when only network changes are required. • Expanded Account Management: – Linux: Adds support for setting the root account password during customization. – Windows/Linux: Adds support for resetting root (Linux) and Administrator (Windows) passwords on existing VMs. – Script Execution: Enhanced support for executing customization scripts within Windows guest environments. • For Powered-On VMs: – Live Network Customization: The Guest Customization API supports updating guest network settings for powered- on VMs. This capability facilitates real-time network change

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: and enhances operational flexibility. Strategic partnerships with Hugging Face and participation in key open source consortiums (PyTorch, OPEA, UALink) ensures optimized performance and accelerate AI adoption. Support for Intel NIC E825 for Xeon Gen 6 (Granite Rapids-D): ESX 9.1 expands hardware compatibility by adding support for Intel NIC E825, which is integrated into the Intel Xeon Gen 6 (Granite Rapids-D) platform and functions as a LAN on Motherboard (LOM) NIC. IOMMU Virtualization Support for AMD Hosts with DirectPath Devices: ESX 9.1 introduces IOMMU virtualization support for AMD hosts with DirectPath devices to deliver significant performance gains and near-native hardware access for critical virtual machines while improving security and memory isolation. Security Quick Boot Support for ESX Hosts with Confidential VMs: With ESX 9.1, Quick Boot supports ESX hosts running confidential VMs, bypassing the time-consuming full power cycle and firmware initialization required by a regular reboot. Quick Boot support speeds up maintenance operations and significantly reduces the upgrade time for the ESX server. User-Level Monitor (ULM) for Enhanced Security: VCF 9.1 introduces Use

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Support for all VM types: ESX 9.1 removes limitations for the types of VMs you can power on in memory tiered host, such as low-latency, security, and monster VMs. Resiliency for Memory Tiering by Using Software RAID Mirroring: ESX 9.1 improves resiliency for Memory Tiering by allowing for a mirrored device, without impacting performance. With the enablement, if a device fails, the secondary device performs memory tiering to ensure continued operations. With a second NVMe device using RAID1 mirroring, memory tiering cannot fail, regardless of NVMe device lifetime, reliability, and wear-out. vCenter New Features File integrity monitoring capability: VCF 9.1 adds a File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) capability, aligned with NIST SP 800-115, which detects changes by malware or malicious actors to static files or binaries installed by vCenter. FIM is turned on by default and runs every four hours to detect malicious or unintentional changes or corruption of the installed files . You can get a FIM report by using API or stream FIM logs to VCF Operations by using the syslog service. Pre-installed Logs Management Agent: vCenter 9.1 comes with a pre-installed Logs management agent that pushe

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Starting with VCF 9.1, vSphere Lifecycle Manager improves remediation efficiency by skipping the transfer of reserved VIBs that are not part of customized ESX images, saving bandwidth and reducing task duration. vSphere Client Memory Tiering Improvements: With vCenter 9.1, the vSphere Client adds the following improvements for Memory Tiering: • Memory pane: – Shows tier 1 stats at host and cluster level in a summary page with a memory card. – Streamlines the configuration at cluster level into a single flow that includes device selection. – Enhances monitoring by plotting graphs with tiering-specific metrics. • Cluster workflows: – Memory Tiering cluster configuration allows for device selection and selection of Memory Tiering parameters. Improved Interface for GPU Passthrough Devices: With vCenter 9.1, the vSphere Client adds enumeration in human-readable form for GPU passthrough devices, simpler names in English, and filtering capabilities to enumerate the passthrough device list. VPC Transit Gateway, Traceflow, and Live Traffic Analysis available from the vSphere Client: With vCenter 9.1, you can configure an external connection for a Transit Gateway, use Traceflow to inspect th

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Event Tracking: VCF 9.1 adds an Event Tracking page in the vSphere Client, which visualizes the full sequence of events when an ESX host fails and HA initiates recovery actions. The page provides a clear timeline that correlates ESX host failures, failover decisions, restarts, and recovery steps, all in one console. For more information, see Monitor and Analyze High Availability Events. vCenter Common objects identifiers between NSX and vCenter: Starting with VCF 9.1, the maximum length of the Folder object name is increased from 80 tо 255 chars for compatibility with NSX network management to enable common object identifiers between NSX and vCenter. In previous releases, NSX folder names could be too long for vCenter. For more information, see Folders in the Hierarchy. Enhanced scalability in extra-large environments: VCF 9.1 allows for up to 20% faster vCenter operations per minute. As a result, management tasks on virtual machines and ESX hosts, such as power on/off, run significantly faster. Content Library Convert Subscribed Content Library to Published Local Library: With VCF 9.1, to avoid loss of content in case the original published local library is not available, you can

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: With VCF 9.1, vCenter supports OAuth 2.0 API tokens, which help you manage API clients and emergency access clients to support secure automation. For more information, see Managing API Clients and Tokens. Tech Preview of FIPS API: VCF 9.1 launches a tech preview of an API that allows you to retrieve information about the FIPS cryptographic modules used by each VCF component, including version number, CMVP certificate, and CAVP certificate. For more information, see Using the FIPS API to Query FIPS Status of Components. Query API for Efficient Data Access: VCF 9.1 adds a new Query API as extension of the already existing Search Index API that delivers a fast, flexible, and scalable way to retrieve vSphere inventory data such as virtual machines and clusters. The Query API enables: • Server-side filtering based on user-defined criteria • Efficient data access with pagination, property selection, and projections • Lightweight, stateless queries without server-side state or client-side caching • Entity counts and filtered results without retrieving full datasets Designed for high-frequency automation use cases, the Query API simplifies data access. Single, unified API endpoint for mana

text: diagnostics and in the VM RuntimeInfo file, under the parameter consolidateWorkTotal . For more information, see Virtual Machine Snapshots Health inVCF Operations Health and Diagnostics. Metrics vMotion Downtime Estimates with Enhanced DirectPath: In vCenter 9.1, configuring a workload with an Enhanced DirectPath device provides you with increased visibility into the estimated downtime for a vSphere vMotion operation, enabling better workload planning and management. GPU Power Metrics for Virtual Machines: With vCenter 9.1, the Advanced Performance charts include Power Capacity Usage - GPU metrics for real-time insights into GPU power consumption at the workload level for virtual machines using NVIDIA GPUs. Distributed Resource Scheduler Intel QAT Offload for Encrypted vSphere vMotion Operations: • CPU Efficiency: QAT offloads the majority of encryption cycles, reducing Source CPU utilization by nearly 67% and reducing Destination CPU utilization by nearly 22%. • Predictability: With QAT, you see significantly lower variance in 'Total Migration Time' and 'Oracle Performance during vMotion' metrics as compared to the software-based encrypted vSphere vMotion that is sensitive to the

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: In VCF 9.1, vSAN clusters use RAID-6 as the default RAID level. vSAN automatically configures RAID-6, removing the need for manual selection and ensuring superior data protection without impacting performance. For more information, see Selecting the Best RAID Configuration for a vSAN Storage Cluster. Cyber Recovery vSAN Storage Clusters: VCF 9.1 introduces a cyber recovery vSAN storage cluster that you can deploy through vCenter Quickstart. It optimizes recovery from a cyber event using VCF Protection and Recovery and provides alerts to ensure that the cyber recovery cluster is healthy and secure. For more information, see Deploy a vSAN ESA Storage Cluster for Cyber Recovery. Seeding for vSAN Replication: VCF 9.1 optimizes data transfer by using existing replicas to avoid full synchronization. When you create new replications, this seeding capability saves time and bandwidth by syncing only incremental changes. For more information, see Use vSAN Snapshots and Replication. Tag Based VM Membership for vSAN Protection Groups: VCF 9.1 simplifies administration by allowing you to define protection group membership using VM tags. This automation protects new workloads instantly based on

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: CNS/CSI Scalability: VCF 9.1 increases CNS/CSI scalability to 50,000 volumes per vCenter, supports shared-disk clustered workloads on Supervisor Clusters, and adds unified monitoring of Persistent Volume Claims through VCF Operations. Improved vSAN File Services Performance for SMB Workloads: VCF 9.1 improves vSAN File Services performance, delivering up to 2 times faster metadata operations optimized for SMB workloads. For more information, see vSAN File Service. Clustered Applications Support on vSAN: VCF 9.1 extends vSAN support for clustered applications and provides the capability to expand VMs with clustered disks without shutting down the vSAN cluster. This capability supports both Oracle RAC multi-writer and shared VMDK in Windows Server Failover Clusters. For more information, see Clustering Applications on vSAN. vSAN Stretched Storage Across vCenter Instances: VCF 9.1 adds flexibility by allowing vSAN stretched storage clusters to provide storage across vCenter boundaries. This includes sharing storage across workload domains within a VCF install or across multiple VCF deployments. For more information, see Mount Remote vSAN Datastores. Shared vSAN Storage Cluster Support

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: patch or upgrade the components in the workload domain before adding a vSphere cluster. All workload domains that share an NSX Manager with a workload domain without a vSphere cluster cannot be patched or upgraded. For more information, see Create a New Workload Domain without a vSphere Cluster Using VCF Operations. Starting with VCF 9.1, the following configurations are supported for convergence and import operations: • Existing VMware vCenter 8.0 Update 3а or later environment with NSX Manager 4.2 or later, without manual component upgrades. • Existing VMware vCenter 8.0 Update 3а or later environment without NSX Manager, only by manually upgrading your vCenter instance to version 9.1. • vCenter instances without ELM and with existing NSX Local Managers that are part of an NSX Federation configuration. • If an existing VCF instance is configured to use dual-stack (IPv4 and IPv6) networking, existing vCenter environments that use dual-stack networking. Default Component Deployment Starting with VCF 9.1, VCF Installer deploys a default set of VCF management services components during new deployment. The components included are VCF services runtime, fleet lifecycle, identity broker,

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: NSX This document contains the following sections • Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Networking • Edge Platform • Enhanced Data Path and Performance • Platform Security • VCF Integration • Network Visibility • Network Observability • IP Address Management • Load Balancer • Operations • VKS Multi-Interface Networking • VCF Brownfield/Import • Installation and Upgrade - LCM • Routing Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Networking AVI Integration: VPC and Distributed Transit Gateway (DTGW) with Distributed VLAN Connections This release introduces support for AVI load balancers with VPCs and and Transit Gateways with distributed VLAN connection. For more information, see the Avi Load Balancer documentation. Distributed VLAN connection support for Supervisor and VCF Automation By integrating the Virtual Network Appliance (VNA), we’ve added the essential services needed to enable Distributed Transit Gateway for the Supervisor. This capability allows VCF Automation environments to connect to the data center using only a simple VLAN removing the need for more complex networking prerequisites. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VPC Services: Load Balancer on Virtual Network Appliance VPCs now support Load Balancer (LB) functionality through a solution deployed on top of a Virtual Networking Appliance (VNA). Layer 4 (L4) load balancing service is fully supported and runs within this dedicated VNA ensuring scalability, flexibility, and enhanced service isolation within your virtual network infrastructure. VPC Services VPN support on Centralized Transit Gateway IPSec VPN services support for VPC using centralized external connectivity - With VCF 9.1, IPSec VPN service is now supported for VPC using centralized external connectivity enabling secure and encrypted communication across diverse network environments. The release includes support for both Policy-Based and Route-Based (static route only) VPNs offering flexibility to meet various connectivity and security requirements. For more information, see Enabling an IPSec VPN Service on a Centralized Transit Gateway. 1:N SNAT service support for VPC with external distributed connectivity VCF 9.1 added support for 1:N SNAT when using the distributed Transit Gateway. This feature enables multiple internal VMs to share a single external IP address through Port Ad

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Automation networking 9.1 allows a tenant to have: • Distributed Connection to VLAN for both TGW and direct VLAN extension • Multiple Transit Gateways with multiple connections • Self Service NAT, VPN, Grouping, Firewalling (Stateful GFW and DFW requires vDefend Firewall License) • Create subnets shared across Namespaces. Support of native Infoblox integration with VPC VCF networking IPAM can now optionally integrate with Infoblox to: • Discover Infoblox Network Views, DNS Views, and Network Containers • Use Infoblox Network Containers to create subnets and update VM IP/FQDN information You can configure the feature in NSX: pairing with Infoblox, External IP Block to Infoblox Network Container mapping that can be discovered and used as VCF Automation External IP Block. For more information, see Register Infoblox NIOS DDI with NSX. Ability to define Span for Transit Gateway and VPC The span of VPCs can now be defined directly from vCenter or from NSX letting VPCs be restricted to specific clusters or have them span across all vCenter instances of the networking domain. For more information, see Add a Network Span. New VPC Connectivity Policy VPC communication can now be easily d

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: This release introduces an asynchronous queue-based hardware steering engine to increase flow programming rates and scalability for UPTv2 and Enhanced DirectPath I/O. This capability is available on NVIDIA Accelerated NICs (ConnectX-6 DX, ConnectX-7, BlueField-2, BlueField-3). Uniform Passthrough (UPT) for Accelerated NICs Uniform Passthrough (UPT) enables near line-rate networking performance through the standard VMXNET3 interface by offloading the data path to NVIDIA ConnectX-7 or BlueField-2, BlueField-3 (NIC mode) hardware. This technology removes the requirement for proprietary guest drivers and supports core features like vMotion making it ideal for high- performance general workloads that require operational flexibility and simplified image management. • Best Use Case: High-performance general enterprise workloads that require a balance of speed and operational flexibility. • Key Benefit: High-speed throughput without sacrificing vMotion or guest OS portability. For more information, see Enhanced Network Offloads and Advanced NIC Passthrough. Enhanced Direct Path I/O for Accelerated NICs Enhanced direct path I/O (EDPIO) delivers the highest possible throughput and lowest lat

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: New password to be different from the old password in case of the Reset Password operation in NSX Starting with VCF 9.1, a user cannot provide the old password during the Reset Password operation of a local account. For information about resetting password, see Resetting User Passwords. Make regular user accounts created by NSX internal accounts on ESXi Starting with VCF 9.1, NSX no longer creates and uses the following regular users on ESX hosts for its own operations: mux_user, da-user, nsx-user, and lldpVim-user. Instead, it uses special accounts not visible to users. NSX Transition to Ubuntu 24.04 and Chiseled Containers All NSX appliances have been upgraded to Ubuntu 24.04 that leverages chiseled containers to minimize the attack surface, optimizes deployment efficiency, and simplifies patch management through a unified and enterprise-supported stack. VCF Integration Workflow to make domains VPC-ready In VCF 9.1, you can configure vNetworking service (Tunnel EndPoint - TEP) even if it was not configured initially during creating or importing your VCF domain. You can also configure the default Transit Gateway as part of the default project. Overlay service configuration in vCen

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • IP Address Management (IPAM) improved Provider Control: It is now possible to share the IP Blocks to the tenant while limiting the visibility on the content of the IP Block. For instance, it allows the Provider to share an IP Block internet "Internet" to the Organization in VCF Automation with a quota of two IPs without exposing the Internet IPs generally owned by the Provider. • IPAM Allocation, Usage, and Visibility: The IPAM offers enhanced IP allocation and IPAM management capabilities across the UIs consuming VPCs (VCF Automation, vCenter, and NSX) For more information, see Add an NSX IP Address Block. VPC Distributed DHCP Server enhancements VCF 9.1 introduces automatic detection of static IP addresses that are now automatically excluded from the DHCP pool. This enhancement ensures seamless IP address management by preventing conflicts between statically assigned and dynamically allocated IPs, thus improving network stability and reducing manual configuration errors. Load Balancer Distributed Load Balancer decoupling from Distributed Firewall The Distributed Load Balancer is now independently managed and decoupled from the Distributed Firewall (DFW). This separation provide

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Installation and Upgrade - LCM VDS support for VCP Autobootstrap VDS capabilities and enhancements to support the NSX - vSphere Config Profiles integration. Move NSX Edge/SVM Upgrades to the End of Upgrade Sequence to Complete Aligning NSX Upgrade Process with vSphere • Easier and faster upgrades - Consolidated maintenance windows for NSX and vSphere management plane and data plane. • Latest VMX version on your NSX Edges as part of the upgrade process. No post upgrade manual intervention or extra reboot needed. DPDK Upgrade to 24.11 Upgraded Data Plane Development Kit (DPDK) to version 24.11, bringing performance improvements, enhanced stability, and support for the latest hardware and features. This ensures better efficiency and future compatibility for high- performance networking workloads. For more information, see NSX Bare Metal Requirements. Routing Dynamic BGP Peering for Auto-Scaling applications This feature provides dynamic BGP peering support on Tier-0 gateways and Tier-0 VRFs. With this feature, users can define a range of IP addresses that will be used to determine when a Tier-0 gateway should establish BGP peering with external routers. For more information, see Confi

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Operate – Real-time metrics – Findings – Organizations – VCF health – Log management – VCF Operations for networks – Storage operations – Workload monitoring and observability • Protect – Security and compliance VCF Operations UI Experience VCF Operations now features an enhanced, intuitive experience structured around the functional pillars of VCF: Build, Manage, Operate, and Protect the private cloud. All capabilities are aligned with these operational pillars. Logging in to VCF Operations While logging in to VCF Operations, use https://<IP> or FQDN. Build The Build capability provides the ability to deploy and manage the lifecycle of your private cloud. It orchestrates the complete platform lifecycle from the initial deployment, converging your existing infrastructure into the VCF private cloud, to the ongoing lifecycle management of your private cloud by providing the ability to patch and upgrade your infrastructure. Beyond deployment and lifecycle capabilities, this workspace acts as the central hub for managing software binaries using the software depot and running lifecycle updates to keep the platform current. It also enables extensibility by providing developers with acc

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • VCF services runtime For more information, see Deploy Log Management, Deploy VCF Operations for networks, Deploy Real-time metrics, Configure Backup Schedules for VCF Automation and VCF Management Services, In Place Restore of VCF Management Services Components, Performing Post Deployment Actions on Components, Scale up VCF Management Components. Domain lifecycle management Optimized NSX Manager and vCenter Maintenance Window You can schedule an update scenario that reduces the required maintenance window downtime. The vCenter reduced downtime update (RDU) preparation stage is performed first and does not require downtime. Then, in the maintenance window, the NSX Manager nodes are updated followed by the vCenter instance switchover stage. For more information, see Perform an Optimized Upgrade of vCenter and NSX Manager. NSX Edge Upgrades at the end of Domain Upgrade Sequence NSX Edge clusters are now upgraded at the end of the domain upgrade process, following the upgrade of the management domain components and ESX clusters. This reordering aligns the NSX upgrade process with vSphere best practices, ensuring NSX Edges are upgraded on the hosts with the expected version. The NSX f

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: SDDC Manager Scale Manage a maximum of 5000 hosts per VCF Instance • Per Instance level number of hosts scale support has been increased by 2x from VCF 9.0. • You can now perform streamlined management of DNS, NTP at scale. • Parallelization of operations has been enhanced, allowing for 256 simultaneous cluster upgrades and concurrent workflows for domain creation and host commissioning. • Revamped UI and API for brownfield imports and pre-checks to perform these operations in a scaled environment. Manage The Manage capability acts as the administrative control plane dedicated to maintaining fleet hygiene, governance, and resource efficiency. It streamlines complex operational tasks by unifying certificate lifecycle management, identity and access controls, and license entitlement into a centralized workflow. Along with fleet governance, it also provides financial and operational insights through capacity planning and cost metering tools, ensuring the environment is both efficient and cost-effective. License management Automatic License File Download in Connected Mode If your VCF Operations is in connected mode, a new license file is now automatically downloaded to VCF Operations e

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • SSO-Centric Management: Manage the VCF SSO lifecycle at the identity broker level rather than from a VCF Instance for better operational granularity. • vCenter Role Sync and Remediation: Provision vCenter custom roles across multiple vCenter instances and VCF Instances. VCF Operations automatically detects and remediates configuration drift. For more information, see Provisioning vCenter Custom Roles. • OAuth 2.0 API Tokens: Help users with appropriate permissions to generate standard OAuth 2.0 tokens for VCF components. As an admin, you can also manage API clients and emergency access clients to support secure automation. For more information, see Managing API Clients and Tokens. • Migration from embedded to instance deployment of the identity broker: Support for the migration of the identity broker from embedded mode to instance mode in VCF Operations. For more information, see Migration of Identity Broker (Embedded) to Identity Broker (Instance). • Migration from VMware Identity Manager to Identity Broker: Support for script-based migration of data from VMware Identity Manager to identity broker. For more information, see Migrating VMware Identity Manager to Identity Broker. •

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Configuration management configuration templates are now available for vSphere Configuration Profile (VCP) enabled clusters in VCF Operations. You can monitor, detect configuration drift, and schedule drift detection on ESX Clusters across your VCF Fleet. You can also identify, view, and download host and cluster drift information from VCF Operations. For more information, see Creating Configuration Management Templates for Clusters and Detecting Drifts and and Downloading Drift Reports for Clusters. Centralized vCenter Tag Management from VCF Operations VCF Operations offers the following features: • You can assign tags on any object across vCenter instances in your VCF fleet from VCF Operations. • You can view and manage all your vCenter tag assignments across the VCF fleet in a single pane of glass in VCF Operations. For more information, see Tags and Categories Overview. Fleet Settings You can configure the following: • Password Policies For more information, see Create a password Policy for VCF Components. • IAM Settings For more information, see Identity and Access Management Settings. • DNS and NTP Settings – VCF Operations now has a consolidated interface for DNS/NTP settin

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Operations now delivers comprehensive costing capabilities across your entire Kubernetes ecosystem, including nodes, clusters, vSphere namespaces, projects, and organizations. For more information, see Cost Overview. Chargeback • Upfront Pricing: VCF now delivers real-time pricing estimates when you deploy virtual machines and Kubernetes nodes in VCF Automation. For more information, see Configuring Provider Pricing Details. • Tenant Reports and Alerts: – Immediate email notifications to tenants regarding capacity thresholds, pricing and other relevant information across organizations and regions. – Comprehensive reporting of infrastructure consumption, resource utilization, and cost breakdowns tailored by the providers for tenants and delivered directly to the tenant’s email. For more information, see Creating Notification Rules for Tenant in VCF Operations. • Bill PDF Export and Emails: Streamlined billing communication with automated PDF delivery that makes it easier for tenants to access, review, and manage their bills anytime and from anywhere. For more information, see Download and Publish Bills. • VKS Chargeback: VCF Operations now offers powerful tools to implement tran

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Operations orchestrator Repository improvements for Python and PowerShell environments You can now configure up to two repositories for your Python and PowerShell scripting environments. This allows you to achieve a more efficient separation between development and production code. Improvements to the default error handler Previously, the default error handler workflow element could only catch and handle errors in the main workflow elements of the workflow schema. With VCF 9.1, the default error handler can now also catch and handle errors in the default error handler element itself. Users can also reconfigure the number of re-entries that the default error handler can perform when it encounters an error. These improvements enhance the debugging and recovery functionality for workflow runs. Configurable session timeout Administrators can now configure the session timeout of the VCF Operations orchestrator UI in minutes. By configuring a custom session timeout, users and workflow developers can avoid losing unsaved changes because of a short timeout limit. This change is only applicable to external VCF Operations orchestrator instances. Session timeout for embedded VCF Operation

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • VCF Operations HCX Management Pack: A new management pack is available for installation in VCF Operations, enabling critical services like password rotation for local users and certificate rotations, log bundle collection. Note: This pack is incompatible with the legacy vRealize Operations management pack for HCX. • Identity and Access: Support for VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) and VCF Roles for unified authentication and authorization on the HCX Manager Web UI and API. Migration and Interconnect Enhancements • WAN Optimization: Re-introduction of an efficient WAN optimization functionality embedded directly within the HCX Interconnect service mesh configuration. This feature is exclusive to enhanced service meshes and is not available for OSAM and HAV migrations. • Local Site Migrations: Support for appliance-less migrations within a single vCenter instance with HCX Assisted vMotion (HAV), enabling source and destination to be the same instance. • Advanced Storage and Rules Support: – Support for non-default storage policies in Cold migrations. – Ability to select multiple destination datastores for a single virtual machine (VM), except for OS Assisted Migration (OSAM). • VLAN Networ

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1

Findings Log Assist enhancements VCF Operations health and diagnostics 9.1 adds automatic log bundle generation for the following VCF components: • Full support bundle for the VCF Operations cluster • VCF management services • Cloud proxies • Log management • Identity broker Log Assist 9.1 also allows you to generate bundles for selected VCF Operations nodes, not only the cluster-level bundle option. For more information, see Use Log Assist to Accelerate Your Support Cases.. Public APIs for Findings Starting with VCF Operations health and diagnostics 9.1, you can use a set of public APIs that allow you to integrate diagnostic findings into your internal reporting and monitoring workflows such as risk management or ticketing. The Findings API allows you to centralize monitoring of real-time findings or get data for trend analyses. For more information, see Diagnostics Public REST API. vSphere High Availability metrics in VCF Operations health and diagnostics VCF Operations health and diagnostics 9.1 adds vSphere High Availability metrics that help proactive identification of issues and preventive resolution from a single vie

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Endpoint monitoring, replacing the ping reachable/unreachable check to the vSphere Client and APIs. • Extends the list of vCenter services to 18 from 6. • Adds the VM Operations Task ID parameter that allows a drill down to the backtrace of a particular task. • Adds the Error Stack panel to analyze the logs in case of VM failure. • Highlights VMs with fast-growing snapshot sizes and the biggest VMs in terms of snapshot size. For more information, see vCenter Health in VCF Operations Health and Diagnostics. Monitor NSX health and operational state VCF Operations health and diagnostics 9.1 provides the key metrics for NSX building blocks that help you track points of failure to prevent overutilization and optimize performance in your system. NSX metrics in VCF Operations diagnostics covers all operational aspects of the underlying infrastructure and appliances. For more information, see NSX Health in VCF Operations Health and Diagnostics. Monitor vSAN cluster health and performance VCF Operations health and diagnostics 9.1 gives you a single pane of glass for any vSAN-related monitoring, issue detection, and troubleshooting tasks. Storage Operations is integrated with the Diagnosti

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Network Assessment and Value The Network Assessment and Value feature helps users to evaluate their current network infrastructure and see the value of VCF networking capabilities. Users can benefit by utilizing VPC networks and achieve capital expenditure (CAPEX) and operational expenditure (OPEX) savings. This assessment focuses on deployments with only virtual networking (VDS). Existing VPC networking configurations are not taken into account in the assessment. For more information, see Network Assessment and Value Analysis. Expanded IPFIX Functionality for VKS Clusters This feature allows for the application of container IPFIX flows on VKS clusters deployed with Antrea to be exported to the VCF Operations for networks collector. Flows are activated on all VKS clusters after Antrea IPFIX is enabled on the vCenter data source. Flows are then parsed and aggregated to produce dashboards in VCF Operations. These dashboards focus on traffic patterns, such as pod-to-pod and pod-to-external traffic between clusters. For more information, see Using Antrea IPFIX for VKS Flows. VPC Planning The VPC planning feature provides users with guidance in transitioning from vSphere networking to V

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: reporting aligns with the storage industry and helps promote the benefit of major ESA features. For more information, see vSAN Cluster Summary Tab. Workload monitoring and observability Load Balancing for cloud proxies that are a part of HA enabled collector groups You can now activate load balancing for all cloud proxies that are a part of HA enabled collector groups for both product managed and open source Telegraf agents. For more information, see Monitoring Application Services using Product Managed Telegraf and Monitoring Application Services and Operating Systems using Open Source Telegraf. Monitoring Data Service Workloads with VCF Operations VCF delivers a cloud-native Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS) experience through Data Services. As the unified platform for database provisioning and lifecycle management, Data Services now integrates with VCF Operations to provide out-of- the-box monitoring for these database workloads. For more information, see Monitoring Data Services in VCF Operations. Protect Serving as the platform's security command center, the Protect capability focuses on ensuring cyber resilience and rigorous regulatory adherence. It provides continuous, real-time

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Automation 9.1 introduces vDefend Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall support directly within VCF Automation. Provider administrators can now delegate vDefend Firewall services to organization administrators to control their own vDefend Gateway for the Transit Gateway Firewall and Distributed Firewalls. In addition, out-of-the- box security profiles have been created to allow uniform vDefend Firewall security for new and existing VPCs along with role-based access control labeling for dynamic security groups. For more information, go to the official vDefend Firewall documentation. Setting for default Private VPC and Private TGW IP Blocks Starting with VCF Automation 9.1, providers can use the Provider Management UI to configure a default private VPC and private TGW IP blocks that are configured in the organization itself after the organization's regional networking is set up. These settings can also be overridden for each organization when its regional networking is provisioned. Support for Avi Load Balancer VCF Automation 9.1 introduces full self-service support for Avi Load Balancer. From the provider management portal, administrators can delegate load balancing creation

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Organization Management Change Namespace Allocation Limits In VCF Automation 9.1, application teams can adapt to the changing resource needs of their applications by performing day 2 actions on namespaces like changing resource limits, VM classes, storage classes, and shared subnets. Project Content Libraries VCF Automation 9.1 supports the ability for project teams to maintain content libraries that are specific to their project. This capability gives project teams additional flexibility and removes their dependency on the organization administrator. Project content libraries can also span across multiple projects. See Create a Project Content Library in VCF Automation. Canonical Content Libraries In VCF Automation 9.1, organizations can now have subscribed content libraries for Canonical Ubuntu VM OS images. The Ubuntu LTS images are validated and supported by VCF. Organization administrators or advanced users can synchronize the Canonical subscribed content library to fetch new sets of images. See Subscribe to Ubuntu OS Images from a Canonical Content Library in VCF Automation. Support for Shared NSX Subnets in Organizations VCF Automation 9.1 allows organization administrators

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Introduced the Utilization API, which monitors vCenter capacity and usage metrics. Introduced the Utilization API, which monitors vCenter capacity and usage metrics, including: • The number of active connections compared to the maximum allowed connection limit. • The endpoints through which these connections are established. • The number of requests sent to vCenter services relative to the maximum request capacity supported by those services. Building on this API, new UI alarms have been added. These alarms are triggered when: • Active connections exceed a configured threshold, and/or • Service request volume surpasses the defined limit. Threshold values are fully configurable through Advanced Settings. The vCenter Group Federated API (VGFA) introduces a single unified API endpoint for managing all vCenter instances in a vCenter group. It allows clients to view and manage inventory across multiple vCenter instances as if they were one—without changing existing API integrations. VGFA can be enabled directly from the VCF Operations UI once SSO and vCenter group configuration are in place. After activation, users gain seamless access to a consolidated inventory and operations experien

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: To accelerate your development, we have added a wide array of new samples to the VCF Java SDK. These samples cover: • VCF Installer and vCenter • NSX and VCF Operations • SDDC Management workflows The build system for the VMware Cloud Foundation Java SDK samples is changed from Gradle to Maven. The code samples are shipped as separate .ZIP deliverable along with the VMware Cloud Foundation Java SDK. VMware Cloud Foundation Python SDK New components are now supported with VMware Cloud Foundation Python SDK The list of new components supported in VCF 9.1 : • VMware NSX • VMware Cloud Foundation Operations • Log Management • VMware Cloud Foundation Operations for networks • Fleet Lifecycle • SDDC Lifecycle Additionally, the VODAP OpenAPI specifications are now available. Enhanced samples and API reference documentation To accelerate your development, we have added a wide array of new samples to the VCF Python SDK. These samples cover: • VCF Installer and vCenter • NSX and VCF Operations • SDDC Management workflows The code samples are shipped as separate .ZIP deliverable along with the VMware Cloud Foundation Python SDK. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • vSAN Max: Support for client network enablement. • Remote Datastore Management: Capability to add/remove remote datastores and cross vCenter remote datastores using both desired state and incremental methods. Advanced VPC networking The VPC module receives a massive expansion in 9.1, offering deeper integration for transit gateways and connectivity policies: • DHCP & IP Management: Extended DHCP configurations for VPCs/Subnets and the ability to define IP Blocks for services or external IPs. • Transit Gateway & Connectivity: New tools to manage Transit Gateway lifecycles and Connectivity Profiles to define how VPCs attach to gateways and IP blocks. • Connectivity Policies: Introduced the ability to define logical VPC Groups with specific communication policies: – Community: Inter-VPC communication within the group. – Private: Isolation from other VPCs. – Promiscuous: Communication across all VPCs. • Visibility: New support for retrieving VPC NAT configurations and viewing VPC-specific alarm information. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: With VCF 9.1, AMD Solarflare 8000 and X2 series Ethernet adapters are deprecated and support will be removed in a future VCF release. Deprecation of SHA1-based cryptographic algorithms: Usage and dependencies on SHA1-based cryptographic algorithms are deprecated and will be removed in a future minor VCF release. Marvell E3 and E4 Drivers Deprecation: Support for Marvell's Converged Network Adapters (CNAs) FastLinQ 57800/57810/57811 series and FastLinQ 41000/45000 series is deprecated in VCF 9.1 and will be be discontinued in a future major release. Cisco Virtual Interface Card (VIC) 1200 and 1300 Series Deprecation: Support for Cisco Virtual Interface Card (VIC) 1200 and 1300 series devices is deprecated in VCF 9.1 and will be discontinued in a future major release due to lack of support for Enhanced Data Path (EDP). Other Notes Expiration of Microsoft 2011 Secure Boot Certificates: Microsoft UEFI and KEK CA 2011 certificates, which affect the Secure Boot capability for ESX hosts and Linux guests, expire in June 2026, while the Windows Production PCA 2011 certificate, which affects the Secure Boot capability for Windows guests, expires in October 2026. The expiration of the 2011 ce

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: End of Support Notes Blocking port 514: Starting with vCenter 9.1, the unencrypted port 514 (UDP/TCP) is blocked for use and any syslog data transfers to vCenter by port 514 are no longer supported. You can use port 1514, which supports secure, TLS-encrypted connections, for all syslog traffic to vCenter. If you are using the syslog.ext.port option for scripted CLI installations, you must switch to using the syslog.ext.tls.port option. Removal of vSphere Cluster Services: Starting with vCenter 9.1, vSphere Cluster Services (vCLS) is deactivated by default and you cannot re-activate the capability. All remaining APIs and configurations are not supported and will be removed in the next major release. Other Notes Extended vCenter API that sets the global policy for local accounts: VCF 9.1 extends the vCenter API that sets the global policy for local accounts, (PUT /api/appliance/local- accounts/global-policy ) to change existing user configurations, along with its current functionality to configure password expiration for new users. If you need to set the configuration of a certain user as different from the global policy, you can create an exception by using another local accounts AP

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Operations Deprecation Notes Compliance feature is now deprecated and replaced by Security Posture Management Security Posture Management offers enhanced assessment and remediation capabilities for VCF. For more information, see Security Posture Management. Deprecation of the legacy cluster expansion method in VCF Operations 9.1 In VCF Operations, the legacy method for expanding an existing cluster via the newly deployed node is deprecated in VCF 9.1. Deprecation of Microsoft Azure and Amazon Web Services as data sources in VCF Operations for networks Azure and AWS data sources are now deprecated. Deprecation of bundled OpenSSL in VCF Operations for logs Agent The VCF Operations for logs Agent fails to function on host systems that do not have OpenSSL version 3 or higher installed. Starting with VCF Operations for logs Agent 9.1.0, the OpenSSL library is no longer bundled within the agent package. Instead, the agent has transitioned to using the system's default OpenSSL library to handle cryptographic operations. To ensure the agent functions correctly, you must either update their host system to a version that includes OpenSSL 3 or higher or continue utilizing an older version

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Other Notes Support for Windows log agent VCF 9.1 adds Fluent Bit as a supported version for Windows to collect Windows Event Logs, including System and Security logs. Download the supported Fluent Bit MSI file from here. Support for open source Telegraf agents: VCF 9.1 adds support for the installation of open source Telegraf agents for configurations required for VCF Operations cloud proxies. Support extends to the initial configuration, which provides a consistent flow of metrics to endpoints with no common failure patterns observed on VCF Operations. For queries on agent operations beyond this scope, use the Telegraf open source community. For more information, see Monitoring Application Services and Operating Systems using Open Source Telegraf and Open Source Telegraf Troubleshooting. Important Upgrade Guidance for VCF Operations • Customers planning to upgrade to VCF Operations 9.0.x: Do not upgrade to VCF Operations 8.18.6. Instead, upgrade directly to VCF Operations 9.0.x. • Customers who have already upgraded to VCF Operations 8.18.6: To continue the upgrade path to the 9.x stream, you must wait for a subsequent release, such as VCF 9.1 or VCF Operations 9.0.3, or a later

VMware Cloud Foundation Python SDK Compatibility Notes All modules of the VCF SDK for Python are compatible and verified with the supported versions of Python at the time of their release. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • vCenter Known Issues: – vSphere Client and vCenter Issues – Upgrade Issues – Miscellaneous Issues – Auto Deploy Issues – API Issues ESX Known Issues: Memory Tiering Issues In rare cases, during migration or upgrade some virtual machines in clusters with Memory Tiering and DRS might fail to power on In clusters with Memory Tiering, if Tier-0 (physical DRAM) VMs occupy a significant portion of the DRAM, in rare cases, DRS might fail to place additional VMs correctly during migration or upgrade tasks. Consequently, such VMs might fail to power on after the respective operation. Workaround: Avoid placing non-tiered (DRAM-only) VMs that occupy a significant portion of the DRAM on memory-tiered hosts. In the vSphere Client, you see an alarm that a NVMe Memory Tiering device is not healthy In an ESX host with Memory Tiering and a single NVMe device, if the NVMe device fails and the VM on which the device resides migrates out of the host, the ESX host might also fail. In the vSphere Client, you see an alarm <ESX host> NVMe Memory Tiering device is not healthy suggesting that you need to replace the device. Workaround: Place the ESX host in maintenance mode, replace the NVME device, and u

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 3. Trigger Event: An encrypted VM is powered on, or an operation like vSphere vMotion migration, VM-resume, or Fast Suspend and Resume (FSR) is executed on the encrypted VM while the host is in the low memory state. Workaround: When you power on encrypted VMs or plan operations such as migration, FSR, or VM-resume on encrypted VMs make sure that the ESX host is not in a low memory state that might lead to memory overcommitment. ESX virtual host IP address unexpectedly changes during VM suspend, resume, or snapshot revert operations In some environments, nested ESX hosts using DHCP-based IP configuration might experience unexpected IP address changes during VM suspend, resume, or snapshot revert operations. The issue occurs when NTP time synchronization introduces a significant time drift, causing the DHCP lease renewal to fail before lease expiration. As a result, the DHCP lease expires and the IP address is released and reassigned, leading to a temporary loss of connectivity . Workaround: Use a stable NTP server and configure longer DHCP lease duration and renewal timers to ensure sufficient margin for successful lease renewal during transient network events. You cannot turn on th

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: In a setup of two ESX hosts connected by a Cisco N9K switch, the target node in an RDMA Write operation might trigger an IOMMU fault or a traffic connection timeout. As a result, the target host fails with a blue screen and an error such as PTE not set to allow Write . The issue occurs only under specific conditions: 1. RDMA write operations use DMA-based memory regions (DMA MR) 2. The network QoS of the switch are not properly configured Workaround: None. To prevent the issue, follow these best practices: 1. Use a supported N9K switch model with up-to-date firmware. 2. Properly configure QoS for RoCE on the N9K switch, following the Cisco configuration guides. 3. Avoid using DMA MR–based RDMA write operations. The Cisco nenic driver supports only NVMe over RoCEv2, which uses FRMR for RDMA write operations. Networking Issues Loss of network traffic for VMs connected with Virtual Functions (VF) from NVIDIA ConnectX-7 four ports adapter Due to a hardware limitation of NVIDIA ConnectX-7 four port cards preventing support for hardware LAG, VF configured in LAG might experience traffic loss. Panic" In rare cases, on very large virtual machines, the vpxd service might fail with an out-of-memory condition after around 3 days of uptime. As a result, some running operations in the vCenter instance might fail. The service recovers automatically and vCenter resumes normal operations. Workaround: None In very rare cases, you might see a false positive alarm "License assignment failed for this host" Due to a difference in the license entitlement formats in VCF 9.0 and VCF 9.1 and later, after an upgrade to vCenter 9.1, in the vSphere Client you might see the alarm License assignment failed for this host . This issue is more likely to occur during periods of high activity, such as vCenter processing more than 5,000 parallel operations per hour. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: A change in the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) usage in PostgreSQL can cause the /storage/dblog partition to reach 100% capacity during certain scenarios such as long-running uptime, which might affect vCenter availability or database operations. To avoid such issues, the /storage/dblog partition size is doubled across all deployment sizes in vCenter 9.1. For example, from 25 GB to 50 GB on large deployments. When you perform an in-place upgrade from vCenter 9.0 to 9.1 and later, the former /storage/vtsdblog storage is repurposed and its capacity is added to /storage/dblog , so the dblog partition is effectively doubled. As a result of this repurposing, you see two disks backing /storage/dblog and two disks backing /storage/lifecycle after the update. This is expected and does not require any action. Workaround: None. When you update from vCenter 9.0 to any 9.1 release, the upgrade automatically repurposes / storage/vtsdblog and adds it to /storage/dblog . No additional action is required. text: The virtual machine cannot be reverted when crossing an Improved-Virtual-Disk snapshot. or an error such as: The operation is not allowed in the current state. Workaround: None. The operation is not supported. Auto Deploy Issues Booting stateful ESX hosts on a vSphere Configuration Profile (VCP) cluster fails with error "Error copying / downloading file" When you use Auto Deploy to provision ESX hosts in a VCP cluster at scale, if you do not provide a ready configuration, booting hosts fails with the message Error copying / downloading file . Workaround: Before you start using Auto Deploy with VCP, you must have a ready configuration, either in a .json file or imported from a reference host. The best practice is to: • Create a VCP cluster. • Manually configure the desired settings on an existing ESX host that you select as a reference host. • Import the reference host configuration to create a draft, make changes, precheck, and apply it. Successful provisioning of hosts in VCP clusters requires a valid, non-empty desired configuration document. If the cluster configuration lacks networking details, Auto Deploy rules might fail, because hosts cannot come online. Always validate the

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: When you enable global deduplication on a vSAN ESA cluster, the Deduplication store appears in the Virtual Objects view but does not display its UUID or placement details. The UUID exists in the system and can be verified through command- line tools, but it is not visible in the vSphere Client UI. Workaround: To view the Deduplication store UUID, use the ESXi command line: esxcli vsan dedup dedupstore get This command displays the backend UUID and other Deduplication store details that are not shown in the vSphere Client interface. vSAN ESA with VMware VSS (Volume Shadow Copy Service) quiesced snapshots are inconsistent If you are using vSAN ESA with VMware VSS (Volume Shadow Copy Service) to quiesce your snapshots, from either VMware's own or 3rd-party data protection tools that use VADP, the virtual disks on the running VM might become inconsistent. Specifically, guest files that are deleted only from the snapshot during VSS quiescing may get zeroed out in the running VM. Observed at-risk guest files include edb.log transaction logs and DataStore.edb, both from the Windows Update service. If those files are affected and the Windows Update service restarts for any reason, the prob

text: Workaround: None You cannot use the option "Install on a new vSAN cluster containing the target host" when deploying a vCenter instance Due to a known technical limitation, you cannot use the option Install on a new vSAN cluster containing the target host when deploying a vCenter instance by using the GUI installer. If you use this option, the deployment fails with the error VMware vSAN Health service failed firstboot on the step Install - Stage 2: vCenter Server setup is in progress. Workaround: Do not use the option Install on a new vSAN cluster containing the target host when deploying vCenter. You can activate vSAN from the vSphere Client after the vCenter deployment completes. Mounting remote datastores from vSAN stretched clusters with unsupported vSAN versions is allowed Missing guardrails allow mounting remote datastores from vSAN stretched clusters running unsupported vSAN versions, leading to undesired behavior. Workaround: None. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: When a user modifies the Span field, any previously entered or selected data in the External Connection field is automatically cleared. This behavior occurs specifically when the Connectivity Type is set to Centralized, Distributed, or VXLAN. Woraround: • From UI: Ensure that the Span field value is finalized before entering any information into the External Connection field. • From API: Use Update TGW API to update Span value.PUT: /policy/api/v1/orgs/{org-id}/projects/{project-id}/transit- gateways/{transit-gateway-id} Warning message is missing when tier-0 does not have "Transit Gateway Static advertisement" configured after an NSX 4.2 upgrade. Users configuring a gateway connection and centralized Transit Gateway have no indication that the associated tier-0 is missing TGW_STATIC route redistribution, which leads to a silent routing misconfiguration after NSX 4.2 upgrades. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: the following error message: Failed to bootstrap VCF services runtime. The VCF Installer and SDDC Manager logs show the following error message: Bootstrap failed during stage INIT0001 - Validating configuration: INGRESS_PLATFORM_FQDN XXX could not be resolved via nameserver(s): 127.0.0.1. Workaround: Perform one of the following tasks. • Before starting the convergence process, perform the following tasks. 1. SSH to the vCenter instance. 2. Change to su . 3. Open the /etc/resolv.conf file. 4. Check for more than one line that contains nameserver 127.0.0.1 and remove the duplicate lines. 5. Open the /etc/systemd/network/10-eth0.network file. 6. If DNS=127.0.0.1 or a similar 127.0.0.1 combination is present in the file, delete the 127.0.0.1 entry. 7. Restart the systemd-networkd service by running the systemctl restart systemd-networkd command. • After a convergence to VCF has failed, perform the following tasks. 1. SSH to SDDC Manager component and switch to the root user. 2. Check for more than one line that contains nameserver 127.0.0.1 and remove the duplicate lines. 3. Save the file and retry the failed VCF convergence. • After a convergence to vSphere Foundation has failed, per

Fleet lifecycle service produces workflow inconsistencies due to transient database connectivity issues • Symptom 1: After a VCF deployment workflow completes successfully, certain component deployment tasks or monitoring sub-tasks may remain displayed in an In Progress state in the VCF user interface, even though the deployment has finished and the components are fully operational. This is a display issue only and does not indicate any functional problems with the deployed infrastructure or services. • Symptom 2: During a VCF deployment a component installation is triggered more than once. The subsequent execution of the installation step fails because the component is already installed. • Symptom 3: One or more steps within a VCF lifecycle task display no progress updates for more than two hours, indicating the task is failed or stuck. Workaround: Disregard the error messages. The Binary Management section functions correctly and allows you to download required binaries and complete the deferred component deployment workflow. Converging or importing an existing vCenter instance fails during validation with an error indicating that the NSX- T Manager install image cannot be found for the required NSX version When converging or importing an existing vCenter instance that is not connected to NSX, SDDC Manager automatically deploys a new NSX Manager. The system selects the latest compatible NSX version based on your vCenter version and VCF release. If the install image (bundle) for the selected NSX version is not available in the SDDC Manager depot, the validati

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Workaround: Perform the following tasks. 1. Log in to the virtual machine of SDDC Manager by using SSH, obtain root permissions and run the following command: for s in domainmanager operationsmanager lcm commonsvcs sddc-manager-ui-app; do echo "--- $s ---"; systemctl status $s | grep -E "Active:|Loaded:"; done 2. In the command output, verify that the status for domainmanager , operationsmanager , lcm , and commonsvcs is inactive. 3. Reboot the appliance by running the reboot command. When management domain upgrade process is incomplete, VCF Operations user interface displays incorrect upgrade status for the workload domain When the management domain has pending upgrades, workload domains with fully completed upgrades to the target Bill of Materials (BOM), might display AVAILABLE instead of UP TO DATE upgrade status. Fleet lifecycle incorrectly calculates the upgrade availability, which results in displaying incorrect values in the user interface. The actual upgrade operations through the user interface and API endpoints function correctly and are not affected by the display issue. Workaround: Perform one for the following tasks. • Complete the management domain upgrade to remove a

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: After a successful NSX upgrade, SDDC Manager's inventory and lifecycle management cache might not reflect the updated NSX-T dataplane version due to timeout or synchronization issues. Workaround: The automatic version synchronization process resolves the discrepancy within 24 hours. Alternatively, perform the following tasks to manually trigger version synchronization and update SDDC Manager inventory immediately. 1. Confirm the NSX upgrade completed successfully on NSX Manager. 2. In the /etc/vmware/vcf/operationsmanager/application-prod.properties configuration file, set the check.lcm.isVersionSyncRequired flag to false . 3. In SDDC Manager API, trigger version synchronization by executing a POST request to /v1/resources/version- syncs with resourceType set to SYSTEM . 4. Wait approximately 5 minutes for the lifecycle management inventory refresh cycle to complete. Optionally, trigger a manual lifecycle management inventory cache refresh using POST /lcm/inventory/refresh . 5. Verify the bundle status reflects the correct NSX-T dataplane version by querying GET /v1/bundles/domains/ {domain-id} and checking the nsx_t_dataplane entity status. On environments with enabled Persistent

Workaround: See Broadcom knowledge base article 440200. The Binary Management tab in VCF Operations lists the 9.1 VCF version option, but no 9.1 binaries appear as available for download This is expected behavior at this point, because the upgrade to version 9.1 starts with upgrading your VCF Operations to version 9.1 by using a PAK file. Workaround: To upgrade VCF to version 9.1, strictly follow the upgrade order of components as documented in the 9.1 product documentation: Upgrading to VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1. After a VCF Operations upgrade from 9.0 to 9.1, VCF Automation, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for networks, or VCF Operations for logs might not appear in the VCF Operations UI If VCF Automation, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for networks, or VCF Operations for logs are dele

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Operate vCenter adapter vCenter data collection fails following an upgrade to VCF Operations 9.1 If a vCenter integration was originally configured using an IP address or FQDN that included trailing slashes or sub-paths (for example, vcenter.example.com/, vcenter.example.com/ui ), the collection mechanism in VCF 9.1 fails to initialize. The new version requires a clean IP or FQDN without any trailing slashes or URL paths. Workaround: Prior to upgrading to VCF 9.1, review all existing vCenter integration settings. Verify that the vCenter field contains only the IP address or FQDN, and remove all trailing slashes or trailing URL paths. Log Management vCenter and ESX events or tasks show "Update parameter values" errors approximately every two minutes when there is an HCX Migration Agent (MA) host in the vCenter vCenter and ESX events/tasks display false positive errors such as "Update option values" and "A general system error occurred" when there is an HCX Migration Agent (MA) host in the vCenter. This occurs because the log management component attempts to configure ESX hosts, specifically enabling audit logs by default. But ESX HCX Migration Agent (MA) hosts do not support this op

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 9.0.x, when running in FIPS mode, utilizes a TLS stack that does not send the required SNI extension. Consequently, the gateway returns a default, untrusted certificate to the VCF Operations for logs 9.0 appliance, causing SSO authentication to fail. This issue specifically affects FIPS-enabled deployments; non-FIPS deployments of VCF Operations for logs 9.0.x are unaffected. This issue occurs consistently when VCF Operations for logs 9.0.x is in FIPS mode and VCF Identity Broker has been upgraded to 9.1. Workaround: You can access the VCF Operations for logs 9.0 UI by logging in with a local, non-SSO account. Full SSO functionality is restored after VCF Operations for logs is upgraded to version 9.1, as authentication is then managed through VCF Operations and the updated UI. VCF Operations for Logs Agent version 9.0.x fails to auto-upgrade to newer versions VCF Operations for Logs Agent version 9.0.x is unable to perform automatic upgrades to newer versions. This issue is caused by changes in the versioning scheme and the deprecation of SysV init systems. Workaround: Manually install a newer version of the Log Management Agent. A "Log Store Health Warning" alert appears for healt

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Log collection should only be enabled in one VCF Operations instance for vCenter, NSX or other VCF components Enabling log collection for vCenter, NSX or other VCF component in a new VCF Operations instance overrides the existing configuration of other instances. Log collection should only be enabled in one VCF Operations instance for vCenter, NSX or other VCF components. Enabling it across multiple instances results in undefined behavior where only one instance receives logs at a time. Workaround: None VCF Operations for Logs UI displays an HTTP 500 Internal Server Error when pods are shut down The user interface displays an "HTTP 500 Internal Server Error" when the ops-logs service pods are scaled to zero or shut down. When all ops-logs pods are removed (during maintenance or shutdown), the Envoy Gateway returns an HTTP 500 error instead of the expected HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable). Envoy Gateway proactively detects the lack of healthy endpoints and programs a direct 500 response. If at least one pod exists (even if terminating), the gateway correctly returns 503. Workaround: None. This is cosmetic and does not indicate a functional failure. The service will resume normal opera

If vCenter is upgraded after the real-time metrics component is already deployed, the system fails to enable data collection post- upgrade. Workaround: From the real-time metrics Day-2 actions menu, stop the service and then start the service to resume collection. As a result, these nodes do not appear in the fleet lifecycle inventory and cannot be managed for lifecycle tasks through VCF Operations. For detailed, step-by-step instructions about performing these actions, see Network Inventory. text: the integration hostname matches what the certificate presents, certificate and password management operations should not fail with the TLS handshake failure. In VCF Operations, certificates for a standalone vCenter may not appear if the vCenter TLS certificate does not include a Subject Alternative Name (SAN) that matches the hostname you used when adding the vCenter integration. When you add the vCenter adapter, you can use either an IP address or a DNS name (FQDN). If the vCenter certificate does not have a SAN entry that is used to add the vCenter adapter in VCF Operations, the standalone vCenter certificates are not collected. This is because of HostNameVerification failure when connecting to the vCenter to collect certificates. This validation is enforced by the vCenter SDK and cannot be bypassed. Workaround: Edit the vCenter integration in VCF Operations and set the hostname to the same IP or DNS name that appears in the vCenter TLS certificate’s SAN (the value the certificate is actually issued for). After the integration hostname matches what the certificate presents, standalone vCenter certificates should be collected as expected. NSX certificates are not visible under th

Login works for VCF SSO configured with sAMAccountName. As a result, local roles assigned in VCF Operations orchestrator are ignored during login. By default, the system attempts to fetch entity information every 5 minutes. In high-density configurations, the API requests can overlap and potentially destabilize the environment. Workaround: To reduce the VCF Automation load in large-scale environments, manually adjust the collection intervals and thread pool settings. 1. Use the VCF Operations UI to set the collection interval to 40 minutes and the Advanced Metrics Collection Interval to 240 minutes. 2. Modify the adapter configuration. a. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Deployment, Convergence, and Upgrade Get to VMware Cloud Foundation or VMware vSphere Foundation 9.1. Use VCF Installer and VCF Operations for automated deployment workflows and manually deploy the final components to complete your platform. You can deploy a new VCF or vSphere Foundation platform on pre-installed ESX hosts by using VCF Installer. You can also use your existing virtual infrastructure, in multiple scenarios, as building blocks and converge it to a VCF or vSphere Foundation platform. You can upgrade your existing VCF 5.2.x or 9.0.x VCF environment to VCF 9.1. You can only upgrade your VCF environment to a version that is released after the release date of your current VCF version. See VMware Interoperability Matrix. In This Chapter • Overview of Deploy, Converge, and Upgrade • Components in VCF and vSphere Foundation • Deploying a New VCF or vSphere Foundation Platform • Converging Existing Virtual Infrastructure to a VCF or a vSphere Foundation Platform • Upgrading to VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1 • Upgrading to vSphere Foundation 9.1 • Upgrading vSphere 8 and Aria Operations 8 to 9.1 Getting to Know VCF To apply this documentation, you must be acquainted with the VCF

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Starting with version 9, the deployment process changes and you now install most of the components by using VCF Installer and VCF Operations workflows. Deployment of standalone components by deploying an OVA is typically not supported, apart from specific scenarios where you are explicitly instructed to do so. • For information about the components installed by VCF Installer, see VCF Installer Overview. • For information about the components you install after the VCF Installer workflow, see Completing the Deployment of Your Platform. Converging to vSphere Foundation 9.1 To converge your standalone components to vSphere Foundation 9.1, you follow a specific convergence order. See Converge a vCenter Instance and ESX Hosts to vSphere Foundation. Converging to VCF 9.1 To converge your virtual infrastructure to a VCF fleet or VCF Instance, you follow a specific convergence order for VCF components. Depending on your existing components, proceed with one of the converge scenarios. See Supported Components and Scenarios to Converge to VCF. Upgrading to VCF 9.1 If you have an existing VCF 5.2.x or 9.0.x environment, see Upgrading to VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1. Paths to Building Workload D

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Before you start deploying a new platform, you must deploy and configure the necessary infrastructure. You use the planning section of VCF Installer to gather the information you require to deploy VCF or vSphere Foundation. Alternatively, you can use the Planning and Preparation Workbook. You can also refer to the VMware Cloud Foundation Design for specific requirements and recommendations for the design of each component. Before you deploy a new platform, you must: • Review the prerequisites page in VCF Installer. – Alternatively, review the prerequisite checklist in the Planning and Preparation Workbook. See Planning and Preparation. • Image your ESX hosts. See Preparing ESX Hosts for VCF or vSphere Foundation. • Download the VCF Installer appliance from the Broadcom Support portal. • If the VCF Installer appliance does not have direct access to the Internet, to download VCF installation binaries from the Broadcom online depot (dl.broadcom.com), you download the VMware Cloud Foundation Download Tool from the Broadcom Support portal (support.broadcom.com). Then, you create an offline depot and download the VCF binaries to the offline depot or download and transfer the installation

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. Mount the ESX ISO on the host and restart the machine. 2. Set the BIOS or UEFI to boot from the mounted ISO. 3. On the welcome screen, press Enter to continue. 4. Accept the End User License Agreement by pressing Enter. 5. On the Select a Disk to Install or Upgrade screen, select the drive on which to install ESX on and press Enter. 6. Select the keyboard type for the host. You can change the keyboard type after installation in the direct console. 7. Enter the root password for the host. 8. In the Confirm Install screen, press F11 to confirm the start of the installation. 9. On the Installation Complete screen, press Enter to reboot the host. 10. Set the first boot device to be the drive on which you installed ESX. 11. Repeat this procedure for all remaining hosts. Configure the Network on the ESX Hosts. Configure the Network on the ESX Hosts Use the ESX Direct Console User Interface (DCUI) for host network configuration and administrative access. Perform the following tasks to configure the host network settings: • Configure the network adapter (vmk0) and VLAN ID for the Management Network. • Configure the IP address, subnet mask, gateway, DNS server, and FQDN for the ESX host.

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1

In a web browser, log in to the ESX host using the VMware Host Client. 2. Configure and start the NTP service. a) In the navigation pane, click Manage, and click the System tab. b) Click Time & date and click Edit NTP Settings. c) On the Edit NTP Settings page, select the Use Network Time Protocol (enable NTP client) radio button, and change the NTP service startup policy to Start and stop with host. d) In the NTP servers text box, enter the NTP Server FQDN or IP Address, and click Save. e) Click the Services tab, select ntpd, and click Start. 3. Repeat this procedure for all remaining hosts. Regenerate the Self-Signed Certificate on the ESX Hosts. text: 6. Reboot the ESX host. 7. Log back in to the VMware Host Client and click Services > Disable Secure Shell (SSH) from the Actions menu. 8. Repeat this procedure for all remaining hosts. Configure ESX Hosts with Signed Certificates If corporate policy requires that you use external CA-signed certificates instead of VMCA-signed certificates for ESX hosts, you can manually add external certificates to the hosts. External CA-signed certificate and key are available. When you install ESX software on a server to create an ESX host, the host initially has an autogenerated certificate. By default, when the ESX host is added to a vSphere cluster during deployment of the management domain or other operations involving ESX hosts (for example, host commissioning, workload domain creation, and so on), the autogenerated certificate is replaced with a certificate that is signed by the VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA). When you use external certificates during deployment, they are not replaced by VMCA-signed certificates. After you perform deployment with external certificates for ESX hosts, all future ESX hosts added to VCF must also use external certificates. To deploy the management domain u

Select the TSM-SSH service and click Start if not started. 4. Log in to the ESX Shell for the first host, either directly from the DCUI or from an SSH client, as a user with administrator privileges. 5. Copy the external certificate and key that you want to use to /etc/vmware/ssl. 7. Rename the external certificate and key to rui.crt and rui.key. 8. In the VMware Host Client, select the TSM-SSH service for the ESX host and click Stop. 10. Repeat for all the ESX hosts that you are adding to VMware Cloud Foundation.
