Gather the required information for the software spec that is used to create the custom ISO. a) In VMware PowerCLI, use the Get-DepotBaseImages cmdlet to get the base image version from the zip file for the ESX patch that you downloaded from the patches portal. For example: Get-DepotBaseImages “c:\temp\VMware-ESX-9.1.0-xxxxxxx-depot.zip” b) Use the Get-DepotAddons cmdlet to get the add-on name and version from the zip file for the OEM add-on for ESX that you downloaded from the Broadcom Support Portal. (if applicable) For example: Get-DepotAddons “c:\temp\HPE-9.1.0.10.x-xxx-Synergy-Addon-depot.zip” 2. Create the software spec using the information you gathered in step 1. For example: { "add_on": { "name": "HPE-Custom-Syn-AddOn", "version": "9.1.0.x.x.x-xx" }, "base_image": { "version": "9.1.0-0.xx.xxxxxxxx" }, "components": null, "hardware_support": null, "solutions": null } 3. Deploy VCF Installer Deploy the VCF Installer appliance on a network that can access the ESX hosts and VM management network that you plan to use for the deployment. The appliance must be able to access all required external services, such as DNS and NTP. Before you deploy the VCF Installer appliance, verify that your environment meets the requirements for this process. Resource Requirements • 4 vCPUs • 16 GB RAM • 914 GB storage Network Requirements • Verify that the static IP address, DNS, and FQDN for the VCF Installer appliance are valid and can be resolved. • Verify that connectivity is in place from the VCF Installer appliance and the management VLAN used in the deployment. • Verify that connectivity is in place from the VCF management network to the VCF Installer appliance. Where you deploy the VCF Installer appliance impacts how it can be used. You can deploy the appliance: • Outside of the management infrastructure on any machine

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: If the computer with Internet access can only access the Internet using a proxy server, use the following options when downloading binaries. Option Description --proxy-server, -s Provide the proxy server FQDN and port of the proxy server that the VCF Download Tool uses for Internet access. The required format is <FQDN:Port>. The proxy server must be reachable from the machine where the tool is running. For example: --proxyServer proxy.example.com:3128. --proxy-https Specifies whether the proxy server is configured to be used with HTTPS connections. To use this option, the proxy certificate must be added to the JRE default trust store of VCF Download Tool. The exact path to the /cacerts folder depends on the directory where the VCF Download Tool tar file is extracted. text: Option Description Storage Traffic and NSX Traffic Separation This profile creates three vSphere Distributed Switches with separate physical NICs. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Option Description Route Based on Physical NIC Load (LBT) (Default): the distributed switch monitors the saturation of the physical uplinks. If the primary uplinks exceed a set amout of utilization (75% default) for 30 seconds, the distributed switch moves some VM traffic to another active uplink. Route Based on Source MAC Hash: the distributed switch uses the VM's MAC address and hashes it to an uplink, which creates even distribution for VMs that might change port IDs but keep MAC addresses. Route Based on IP Hash: the distributed switch uses the source and destination IP address of every packet to decide which uplink to use. This allows a single VM to use multiple uplinks simultaneously. Note: You must configure LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol) on the physical Top-of-Rack switches. Use Explicit Failover Order: you define the order in which uplinks are used in case of failure. Active: This traffic type uses the uplink immediately during normal operation. In case of multiple active uplinks, traffic of this type is distributed across all active uplinks simultaneously. Standby: A standby uplink is connected, but no traffic is sent through it as long as at least one "Active"

Health checks do not include VCF Management Services, VCF Automation, and VCF Operations. Note: The health check options are primarily designed to run on the SDDC Manager appliance. Running them on the VCF Installer appliance requires the --force parameter, which instructs the SoS Utility to identify the SDDC Manager appliance deployed by the VCF Installer, and then execute the health check remotely. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Integrating VCF Operations orchestrator by using a management pack To integrate VCF Operations orchestrator, you must install the relevant management pack in VCF Operations. Management packs are hosted on the VCF Solutions Catalog (formerly VMware Marketplace) and can either be downloaded as PAK files or applied directly from Marketplace tab in VCF Operations. For more information on adding and managing integrations in VCF Operations, go to Adding and Managing Management Packs. For more information on using the VCF Operations orchestrator management pack, see Configuring Automation Orchestrator. Configure a VCF Operations Orchestrator Cluster You can configure your new VCF Operations orchestrator deployment to run in high availability by deploying three nodes and connecting them as a cluster. • Download and deploy three standalone VCF Operations orchestrator instances. Note: A clustered VCF Operations orchestrator environment must consist of three nodes. • Configure a load balancer server. A VCF Operations orchestrator cluster consists of three VCF Operations orchestrator instances. To create a VCF Operations orchestrator cluster, you must select one VCF Operations orchestrator ins

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Note: You cannot update the configuration of a cluster node if it has a different root password from the password that is used by the load balancer. To edit the configuration of this node, remove it from the load balancer, edit the configuration and add the node back to the load balancer. To monitor the state of the VCF Operations orchestrator cluster, log in to the VCF Operations orchestrator client and navigate to the System tab of the dashboard. Note: If you are unable to access VCF Operations orchestrator, you can also monitor the states of your cluster nodes by running the kubectl get pods -n prelude command from the VCF Operations orchestrator appliance command line. To check the health status of your load balancer, navigate to https://<load_balancer_FQDN>/vco/api/healthstatus. The health check port is 8008. Deploy VCF Operations HCX VCF Operations HCX is a workload mobility platform that simplifies workload migration across data centers. VCF Operations HCX is an optional component that you can deploy on a VCF Instance domain. • Verify that the VCF Operations HCX. deployment binaries are downloaded to software depot in the VCF Instance where you want to deploy. See Download B

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Import Existing VCF Operations HCX to a VCF Domain Instead of deploying a new VCF Operations HCX you can import an existing instance for VCF Operations HCX version 9.1 or later. The import version must also be the same or earlier than the version of SDDC Manager. • Verify that you have the FQDN and admin user password of your existing HCX appliance. • Verify that you have the IPv4 address and SSL certificate thumbprint, so that you can confirm automatically retrieved information when HCX Manager is imported into the VCF Domain. • Verify that the IP address and Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN) for VCF Operations HCX are resolvable over DNS. • Verify that the existing VCF Operations HCX is located either in the management vCenter instance or in the domain where you plan to import it. • Verify that you have a valid certificate with Common Names (CN) and Subject Alternative Names (SAN). Perform the following steps to import VCF Operations HCX to an existing management or workload domain. Note: If the VCF Operations HCX admin user password is not compliant with the password policy, you must change the password in VCF Operations HCX . 1. Log in to the VCF Operations interface at https:

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: After a successful import, go to the Software Versions tab of the domain. In the Domain Management Components table, verify that the current version and target version of VCF Operations HCX match. Configuring Advanced Architectures for VCF Operations VCF Operations support architectures that are not automated by VCF Installer. You must perform a manual configuration to achieve the required states. Configure VCF Operations Cluster for Continuous Availability VCF Installer supports the deployment of VCF Operations in either simple mode or high availability mode during the initial VCF deployment. After the initial deployment, the VCF Operations cluster can be converted to a continuous availability architecture. Identify three separate fault domains for the installation of the VCF Operations primary node and data nodes, the VCF Operations primary replica node and data nodes, and the witness node, respectively. The fault domains can be located in either a different workload domain, a separate availability zone, or a different region. In a continuous availability architecture, there must be at least one primary node and one data node within the primary fault domain. Additionally, the rep

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Table 952: Key Scenario and Behavior Scenario Behavior Data Storage Data is stored in a primary node and replicated to its paired node in the other fault domain. Loss of One Fault Domain The cluster operates in a degraded state but continues without data loss. The fault domain must be repaired or replaced to restore full functionality. Loss of Two Nodes in One Fault Domain The cluster continues to operate as long as one node from each pair is available. Loss of Connectivity Between Fault Domains One fault domain will go offline automatically. After connectivity is restored, the admin must manually bring the fault domain back online to synchronize data. Witness Node Unavailability If both fault domains are healthy, the witness node’s unavailability has no impact. If communication issues occur, the witness determines which fault domain remains online. Node Replacement Use the "Replace nodes of cluster" functionality in the admin UI. Sync time depends on object count, historical data, network bandwidth, and cluster load. Latency Exceeds 20 ms Synchronization continues but might be delayed. Latency must return to <10 ms for optimal performance. Primary Node Failover If the primary node

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Converging Existing Virtual Infrastructure to a VCF or a vSphere Foundation Platform You can use your existing virtual infrastructure as building blocks for your VCF or vSphere Foundation platforms. The process for converging includes complying with the general requirements and scenario-specific prerequisites, manual upgrades of your existing components, and deployments and configurations performed by VCF Installer. In the supported scenarios, the manual high-level steps upgrade your existing components to version 9.1 and prepare your infrastructure for the VCF Installer workflows. By using the VCF Installer workflows, you can converge the components to a new VCF fleet or a new VCF Instance in an existing VCF fleet. Note: For a VCF platform, the following configurations, requirements, and converge scenarios are applicable for your management domain only. You import and upgrade workload domains in VCF Operations, after you deployed the management domain. The minimum component versions of your existing infrastructure are listed in the specific supported scenarios. See Supported Components and Scenarios to Converge to VCF. Supported and Not Supported Configurations to Converge to VCF

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Category Supported Not Supported • Minimum number of ESX hosts for new deployments of VCF Operations, VCF Automation, and NSX Manager – Simple deployments • Minimum 3 ESX hosts with vSAN Storage or minimum 2 ESX hosts with external storage – High availability deployments • minimum 4 ESX hosts • Any supported vSphere storage type. If the default cluster includes multiple datastore types, VCF determines the primary datastore using the following priority: 1. vSAN 2. NFS v3 3. VMFS 4. NFS 4.1 5. iSCSI 6. vVols* Note: If you converge a cluster that runs vCenter in an environment with multiple mounted datastores of the same type, the datastore on which the vCenter runs is designated as the principal storage for the cluster. If you are using a JSON specification file to converge, you can use the existingDatastor eName property to override the priority order and use a specific datastore. • vSAN Stretched Clusters (ESA, OSA, or ESA storage clusters) with at least 3 ESX hosts in each availability zone and a witness host deployed in a vSAN witness zone that is different from the availability zones • Two-node vSAN clusters for remote offices/branch offices (ROBO) with two ESX hosts and one wit

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Category Supported Not Supported • vCenter instance with temporary IP address • Statically assigned VMkernel IP addresses • Dynamically allocated NSX Host TEP interfaces • Dedicated network for vSphere vMotion • vSphere Distributed Switches (VDS) with enabled link aggregation control protocol (LACP) • ESX hosts with a combination of standard switches and VDS when the VMkernel interfaces are on the VDS • ESX hosts with multiple physical uplinks of minimum of 10Gbps, that are assigned to a vSphere Distributed Switch • ESX hosts with a single Physical Network Adapter (pNIC) with a minimum of 10 Gbps speed, that is connected to a standard switch • Clusters using shared vSphere Distributed Switches • vCenter instances with existing NSX registrations and without ELM • vCenter instances without ELM and with existing NSX Local Managers that are part of an NSX Federation configuration • NSX Bare Metal and VM Edge nodes • Dynamically allocated VMkernel IP addresses Note: You can move to statically assigned IP addresses. See Edit a VMkernel Adapter Configuration. • Multiple NSX Manager instances managing a single vCenter instance • vCenter instances with existing NSX registrations and with EL

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Password Management of imported ESX hosts via the Password Management Console of VCF Operations Note: You can use the workaround in the Broadcom Knowledge Base Article 388859 to create the missing ESXi root credential entries in SDDC Manager to enable password management for these hosts. Supported Components and Scenarios to Converge to VCF Before you start any upgrade or deployment operations on your existing environment, evaluate your environment and decide which supported convergence scenario you want to take. Supported Components The following table lists pre-version 9 components and each table row provides information whether the component can be converged to VCF 9.1. Table 953: Supported Components to Converge by Using VCF Installer Pre-Version 9 Component Converge Existing Component Aria Operations Yes Aria Automation Yes NSX Yes * vCenter Yes ESX Yes vSAN Yes Note: Your pre-version 9 components can be managed by VMware Aria Suite Lifecycle. VMware Aria Suite Lifecycle is not part of VCF 9.1. Post-convergence, the newly introduced Fleet lifecycle and SDDC lifecycle components, part of VCF management services, perform that functionality. * NSX 4.2 or later deployments that

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Converge a vCenter Instance, ESX Hosts, VMware Aria Operations, and Optionally NSX • Converge a vCenter Instance, ESX Hosts, VMware Aria Suite Lifecycle, VMware Aria Operations, and Optionally NSX • Converge a vCenter Instance, ESX Hosts, VMware Aria Suite Lifecycle, VMware Aria Automation, and Optionally NSX • Converge a vCenter Instance, ESX Hosts, VMware Aria Suite Lifecycle, VMware Aria Operations, and VMware Aria Automation, and Optionally NSX After you select a convergence path, depending on the version of your standalone components, you might need to upgrade some of them before you can proceed with converging the components to a supported vSphere Foundation or VCF deployment. For either path, first verify that you meet the minimum version requirements and supported configurations. Converge a vCenter Instance, ESX Hosts, and Optionally NSX To converge your existing vCenter instance and ESX hosts to a management domain, ensure that you comply with the prerequisites, follow the procedure, and perform the next steps. This scenario supports existing environments with and without NSX. • Verify that your existing configuration is supported. See Supported and Not Supported Configu

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: To ensure your VCF environment is fully operational, after completing the required steps in the main convergence procedure, perform the following additional tasks. • License your VCF environment. See Licensing Overview. • Import your existing infrastructure as a workload domain. See Import an Existing vCenter to Create a Workload Domain. • Upgrade your workload domains. See Upgrading VMware Cloud Foundation Workload Domains to 9.1. • Integrate your workload domains in VCF Automation. See Add Workload Domains to Your Cloud Organization Structure. VCF Automation FQDN Enter the FQDN to assign to the VCF Automation instance. Note: Do not use capital letters in the FQDN. VCF Automation VCF services runtime FQDN Enter the FQDN for VCF services runtime to connect VCF Automation with the VCF fleet lifecycle component that manages the lifecycle of VCF Automation. text: Before version 9, VCF always included the following core components: • SDDC Manager • NSX Manager • vCenter • ESX Starting with VCF 9, the management components are part of the VCF fleet. • VCF Operations (previously Aria Operations) • Cloud proxy (previously Aria Operations Cloud Proxy) • VCF Automation (previously Aria Automation, can be deployed as a Day-N operation after the upgrade) • VCF management services (introduced in 9.1) • License server (introduced in 9.1) You begin the upgrade process of your SDDC with the VCF Instance that hosts the VCF Operations instance dedicated to managing your VCF fleet. For information on how to upgrade your workload domains, see Upgrading VMware Cloud Foundation Workload Domains to 9.1. Note: The SDDC Manager UI is being deprecated and will be removed in a future release. After your upgrade to VCF 9.1 completes, use VCF Operations to perform lifecycle management activities. Upgrading a VCF Instance from Version 5.2.x to Version 9.1 You can perform a sequential or skip-level upgrade to VCF 9.1 from VCF 5.2.x. If your platform is at a version earlier than 5.2, you must upgrade the management domain and all workload domains to VCF 5.2.x before y

This scenario takes you to a stable environment that can later be extended to a full VCF environment. See Upgrading to vSphere Foundation 9.1. This procedure is valid only for VCF Identity Broker in appliance mode, see Appliance VCF Identity Broker Model. With VCF 9.1, VCF Identity Broker transitions from being hosted by a dedicated VCF services runtime to being hosted by the shared VCF services runtime, which hosts all VCF management services. Upgrading from VCF 9.0.x If your environment has VCF Operations 9.0.x, the upgrade process manages the transition of lifecycle orchestration from the VCF Operations fleet management appliance to the VCF management services. After you deploy the new cloud proxy, you must configure the cloud proxy to collect data and metrics from SDDC Manager. See Deploy Cloud Proxy as part of a VCF 9.1 Upgrade. You can later shut down and delete the vRealize Operations Cloud Proxy 8.18. Post the upgrade, review the upgraded out- of-the-box-content in VCF Operations, and compare them with the preserved customized content to determine whether to keep your previously modified versions, or to use the upgraded versions. Perform the Upgrade to VCF Operations 9.1 You upgrade VCF Operations 9.0.x and Aria Operations 8.18.x to version 9.1 by using a PAK file in the VCF Operations administration interface. For maintenance upgrades, the PAK upgrade method is not recommended and the user interface suggests you use alternatives. For upgrades from versions 8.x and 9.0.x, PAK upgrade is the only method. • Ensure that you have the VCF Operations fleet management appliance root user password to facilitate the inventory transfer. The process first upgrades the VCF Operations nodes in the cluster and then automatically upgrades the cloud proxy for the VCF Operations instance. If you are upgrading from VCF Operations 9.0.x, the inventory of the VCF Operations fleet management appliance is transferred to the VCF Operations instance. When the transfer completes, the fleet management appliance is decommissioned. V

text: Deploy VCF Operations as Part of a VCF 9.1 Upgrade VCF Operations is a required component in VCF 9 and later. If you do not have an existing deployment of VMware Aria Operations 8.18.x or VCF Operations 9.0.x, you must deploy it before you can proceed with the VCF 9.1 upgrade. You deploy the VCF Operations nodes from the vSphere Client by using an OVA file. After you deploy the VCF Operations nodes, you connect your instance to SDDC Manager to activate the fleet management capabilities in VCF Operations. 1. Download the VCF Operations 9.1 Install OVA file from the Broadcom Support Portal. a) Log in to the Broadcom Support Portal. b) Click My Downloads. c) Search for VMware Cloud Foundation, select VMware Cloud Foundation 9, and click 9.1.0.0. d) Read both terms and conditions to activate the agreement check box and accept the terms. e) For VCF Operations, click View group and download the VCF Operations Install OVA file. 2. Log in to vCenter using the vSphere Client. 3. Start the Deploy OVF Template wizard. text: and uploads it to VCF Operations. Cloud proxies can support multiple vCenter instances. For more information on cloud proxies, see the Cloud Proxy FAQ. • Verify that you have an IP address and a DNS configuration for the cloud proxy. • Verify that you have proper permissions to deploy OVF templates in vSphere. For more information, see Deploy and Export OVF and OVA Templates. • Allow outgoing HTTPS traffic for cloud proxy over port 443. • Allow incoming traffic to cloud proxy over ports 443, 4505, and 4506 for Telegraf based application monitoring. Note: For Telegraf based application monitoring in VCF 9.1 and later versions. Port 8443 will reach end of support after a few releases. It is recommended that you use port 443 so that the end point VM/physical server has access to port 443 of an individual cloud proxy or cloud proxies that are a part of a cloud proxy group. • As a new user, enable port 443 instead of port 8443 so that the end point VM/physical server has access to port 443 instead of port 8443 of an individual cloud proxy or cloud proxies that are a part of a cloud proxy group. • For existing users who upgrade to VCF 9.1, it is recommended that you reconfigure your open

text: 3. Navigate to your vSphere Client, select the name of your vCenter cluster, and select Deploy OVF Template from the Actions menu. 4. Insert the OVA link and then click Next. • Paste the cloud proxy OVA link in the URL field. • Click the Local File option, browse, and select the downloaded OVA file. 5. Follow the prompts to install the OVA on your vCenter. For the most current information about sizing and scaling, see Broadcom knowledge base article 324340. 6. When prompted to enter the Unique Registration Key in the Customize template screen, return to the Install Cloud Proxy page in VCF Operations. 7. Optional: (Optional) Activate Data Persistence to store data in the cloud proxy in case of connectivity issues. For more information, see Activating Data Persistence in Cloud Proxy. 8. Click the Copy Key icon. The unique registration key expires 24 hours after generation. To avoid using an expired key, click Regenerate Key before proceeding. The unique registration key is used by the cloud proxy to authenticate to VCF Operations. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 14. Locate the cloud proxy you installed, select the VCF Operations Cloud Proxy, and click Power on. Note: Power on the cloud proxy within 24 hours of registration. After 24 hours, the Unique Registration Key expires. If the key expires, delete the proxy and deploy a new one. 15. Return to the Cloud Proxy page in VCF Operations to view the status of the cloud proxy you installed. For more information, see Monitoring the Health of Cloud Proxies. 16. After you deploy and configure the cloud proxy, assign the new cloud proxy to a new or existing VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) instance and its associated workload domains. For the existing domains of the same VCF Instance, update the integrations for all vCenter instances to use the new Cloud proxy/ group. After installation, navigate to Operate > Administration > Integrations and edit the VCF instance settings and replace the default cloud proxy with the new cloud proxy. 17. text: Transfer Licenses After You Upgrade VCF Operations from Version 9.0.x Starting with version 9.1, the license server component is mandatory to license a VCF Instance or vSphere Foundation. After the deployment of the license server as part of an upgrade from 9.0.x to 9.1, your licenses are transferred from the VCF Operations instance to the license server. After the deployment of the license server, you might see duplicate licenses in VCF Operations while the licenses are transferred. The duplicate licenses disappear when the transfer completes. During that period, avoid assigning licenses to assets as license assignments might fail. If your VCF Operations instance is in connected mode, the license server is automatically registered and licenses are automatically transferred to the license server. If your VCF Operations instance is in disconnected mode, you must register the license server with the VCF Business Services console. Licenses are then automatically transferred from VCF Operations to the license server. You must generate a new license file from the VCF Business Services console, and import it in VCF Operations. 1. text: Download the NSX Global Manager Upgrade Bundle VCF does not manage the lifecycle of NSX Global Manager instances. To upgrade the NSX Global Managers, you must manually download the NSX upgrade bundle. 1. Log in to the Broadcom Support Portal and browse to My Downloads > VMware Cloud Foundation > VMware Cloud Foundation 9 > VMware NSX > View Group. 2. Locate the NSX version Upgrade Bundle and verify that the upgrade bundle filename extension ends with .mub. The upgrade bundle filename has the following format VMware-NSX-upgrade- bundle-versionnumber.buildnumber.mub. 3. Click the download icon to download the upgrade bundle to the system where you access the NSX Global Manager UI. Upgrade the Upgrade Coordinator for NSX Federation The upgrade coordinator runs in the NSX Manager. It is a self-contained web application that orchestrates the upgrade process of hosts, NSX Edge cluster, NSX Controller cluster, and the management plane. The upgrade coordinator guides you through the upgrade sequence. You can track the upgrade process and, if necessary, you can pause and resume the upgrade process from the UI. 1. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Plan a Management Domain Upgrade To upgrade the remaining core components in your management domain, plan a domain upgrade by setting a target VCF version and VCF component versions, and defining the upgrade sequence for domain components. Starting with VCF 9.1, vCenter and NSX Manager are upgraded in a common step in the upgrade process. You can select between the following upgrade scenarios. • Optimized maintenance window upgrade – Reduces the required time for the maintenance window for the upgrade. The vCenter reduced downtime upgrade preparation stage is performed first and does not require downtime. After this stage is completed, within the same maintenance window, the NSX Manager nodes are upgraded followed by the vCenter instance switchover stage. • Sequential upgrade – Upgrade operations for each component are performed in separate stages in different maintenance windows. The upgrade starts with the NSX Manager nodes upgrade followed by the vCenter instance upgrade. You can select between a vCenter regular upgrade or vCenter reduced downtime upgrade. Note: If you are upgrading from version 9.0 or later, both upgrade options are available. If you are upgrading from version

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Perform Upgrade Prechecks To ensure that your environment is ready for an upgrade, before applying an upgrade, you can perform prechecks in VCF Operations. You can choose which pre-checks to run and either plan pre-check for all components or choose one or more components. You can run general prechecks or prechecks against a specific VCF target version. Alternatively, after you configure a component for upgrade, you can run a component precheck from the dedicated component card. • After you start a precheck of a component, you cannot cancel the precheck and it is not recommended to start or schedule an upgrade. • After a precheck completes, only the latest results are saved. Previous results are not visible. • Even when one or more precheck fails, you can attempt to upgrade the component. The recommendation is to review the errors and warnings, before attempting the upgrade of the component. If you silence a vSAN Skyline Health alert in the vSphere Client, SDDC Manager skips the related precheck and indicates which precheck it skipped. Click RESTORE PRECHECK to include the silenced precheck. Silenced prechecks do not send warnings or block upgrades. 1. Log in to VCF Operations at h

To see all prechecks and precheck details, select EXPORT FULL REPORT. The exported report is in CSV file format and includes all precheck results. VMware by Broadcom 2231

Optional: If the domain contains a host that includes pinned VMs, the precheck fails at the Enter Maintenance Mode step. If the host can enter maintenance mode through the vCenter UI, you can suppress this check for NSX and ESX in VCF by following the steps below. 1. Log in to SDDC Manager by using a Secure Shell (SSH) client with the user name vcf and password. 2. Open the /opt/vmware/vcf/lcm/lcm-app/conf/application-prod.properties file. 3. Add the following line to the end of the file: lcm.nsxt.suppress.dry.run.emm.check=true lcm.esx.suppress.dry.run.emm.check.failures=true 4. Restart the lcm service by typing the following command in the console window. systemctl restart lcm 5. After the lcm service is restarted, run the precheck again. Upgrading

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: NSX Edge Cluster Upgrade During Upgrade After Upgrade • During the edge upgrade, you might experience the following traffic interruptions: – North-south datapath is affected if the edge is part of the datapath. – East-west traffic between tier-1 routers using edge firewall, NAT, or load balancing. – Temporary Layer 2 and Layer 3 interruption. • Configuration changes are not blocked on NSX Manager but might be delayed. • The hardware versions of edge node VMs change or remain the same as follows: – For edge nodes auto-deployed through the NSX Manager UI or the API, the VM hardware is automatically updated to the maximum version supported by the underlying ESX host. – For edge nodes manually deployed through vSphere Client, the VM hardware remains unchanged at the same version. For more details, see Broadcom knowledge base article 319091. • Configuration changes are allowed. • You cannot configure new features introduced by the upgrade until all the edge and host nodes are upgraded. • Run post checks to make sure that the upgraded edge cluster and NSX do not have any problems. text: Manage Unsupported Features Certain features in the source version of NSX might not be supported in the target version. If your deployment contains an unsupported feature, make the required changes to your system configuration before starting the upgrade. The upgrade precheck step of the upgrade process returns an error when it detects an unsupported feature. Multi-NSX Starting with VCF 9.0, the multi-NSX feature is not supported. Before the upgrade, turn off the multi-NSX feature for all registered compute managers, else the upgrade precheck step returns an error. Alternatively, you can map the NSX Manager instances to individual vCenter instances before upgrading. Compute Managers Without Service Accounts Starting with VCF 9.0, a service account is created on vCenter for NSX to communicate with a registered compute manager. If you are upgrading to VCF 9.0 or later and a registered compute manager lacks an enabled service account, a pre-check error is thrown. To proceed with the upgrade, you must enable the service account for those compute managers. Note that for compute managers that already have a service account feature enabled, the same service account is used post upgrade fo

text: The upgrade precheck process in the NSX Manager upgrade coordinator does not detect mixed-mode IP allocations or warn you of the presence of this unsupported condition. Before the upgrade, verify whether mixed-mode IP allocations exist in your environment. If present, use the Promotion feature in the source NSX deployment to migrate the mixed-mode IP allocations to Policy equivalents. Replace member types related to grouping Certain member types are deprecated or no longer supported. If your source deployment uses any of these member types, replace them with their supported equivalents which are functionally the same. Deprecated or Unsupported Member Type Supported Equivalent IPSET (deprecated) Group Logical Switch (not supported) Segment Logical Port (not supported) Segment Port Remove any remaining Manager objects after the upgrade After the upgrade, NSX Manager will raise an alarm if it detects any pure Manager logical objects that have not been migrated to Policy equivalents. text: • When the edge upgrade step begins, the pre-checks validate the peers of each candidate node to be upgraded. If a peer itself has recently been upgraded and is still starting up, the upgrade process implicitly waits for the peer to become available before retrying the pre-check. Deactivate Edge Failover Pre-checks To deactivate some or all edge failover pre-checks, use the API call PATCH https://<NSX Manager>/policy/api/ v1/system-config with a request payload consisting of key/value pairs as described in the following table. By default, the EdgeFailoverReadiness_DisablePreChecks key is set to false , which activates all edge failover pre-checks. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. Log in to VCF Operations at https://<vcf_operations_fqdn> with a user assigned the Administrator role. 2. Select Build > Lifecycle and click your management domain. 3. On the Upgrade tab, under VMware vCenter Preparation Stage, click Configure. 4. Configure the preparation stage. a) Review the upgrade mechanism information and click Next. A vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade (RDU) uses a migration-based approach. In this approach, a new vCenter appliance is deployed and the current vCenter data and configuration is copied to it. During the preparation phase of a reduced downtime upgrade, the source vCenter instance and all resources remain online. The only downtime occurs when the source vCenter instance is stopped, the configuration is switched over to the target vCenter, and the services are started. The downtime is expected to take approximately 5 minutes under ideal network, CPU, memory, and storage provisioning. b) Select a backup option and click Next. Best practice is to have a file-based backup of the vCenter appliance. See On-Demand Back Up of vCenter. After taking a backup, do not make any changes to the vCenter inventory or settings until the upgrade completes successfu

See Monitoring VMware Cloud Foundation Upgrades. If you cancel the switchover, the configuration reverts to the source vCenter instance. • Allow some time (approximately 15 minutes) for post-upgrade auto-reconciliation of configuration drift fixes before scheduling another upgrade. If you schedule a second upgrade to run before the remediation task completes, the upgrade might be cancelled or auto-reconciliation might fail. • Use the vSphere Client to change the vSphere DRS Automation Level setting back to the original value (before you took a file-based backup) for each vSphere cluster that is managed by the vCenter. Click Upgrade Now or click Schedule to upgrade later. When all NSX Manager nodes are upgraded successfully, a message with a green background and check mark is displayed. Note: NSX is not upgraded until all ESX hosts in all vSphere clusters that share an NSX Manager are upgraded to 9.x and the NSX finalize procedure completes. 6. Under VMware vCenter Upgrade, click Configure. Note: If you are upgrading from version 9.0 or later, both upgrade options are available. If you are upgrading from version 5.2.x, the only available option is vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade. vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade A vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade (RDU) uses a migration-based approach. In this approach, a new vCenter appliance is deployed and the current vCenter data and configuration is copied to it. During the preparation phase of a reduced downtime upgrade, the source vCenter instance and all resources remain online. The only downtime occurs when the source vCenter instance is stopped, the configuration is switched over to the t

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 3. Select a time when the vCenter upgrade starts and click Next. 4. Review the upgrade configuration and click Finish. 7. Optional: Click Run Prechecks to run the upgrade prechecks. 8. If you selected the vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade mechanism, click Schedule. a) Schedule a time for the vCenter preparation stage. You can start the preparation immediately or specify another time. b) Specify the switchover behavior after the target instance is created with the selected configuration.Automatic: Proceed with an automatic switchover. • Automatic: Proceed with an automatic switchover. • Manual: You must manually click Switchover Now to start the switchover. • Scheduled: Specify a time for the switchover. 9. If you selected the vCenter Reduced Downtime Upgrade mechanism and the Manual switchover option, under VMware vCenter Upgrade, click Switchover Now. If you cancel the upgrade, the configuration reverts to the source vCenter instance. The upgrade of the vCenter instance starts and you can monitor the progress. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. From your browser, log in as a local admin user to an NSX Manager at https://nsx-manager-ip-address/ login.jsp?local=true. 2. Select System > Upgrade. 3. Verify that the overall upgrade version, component version, and initial and target product version are accurate. a) Optional: Inspect the Dashboard, fabric hosts, edge cluster, transport nodes, and all logical entities. Ensure that all status indicators are green, normal, deployed, and do not show any warnings. b) Optional: Verify the status of several components. • Fabric nodes installation • Transport node Local Control Plane (LCP) and Management plane agent connectivity • Routers connectivity • NAT rules • DFW rules • DHCP lease • BGP details • Flows in the IPFIX collector • TOR connectivity to enable the network traffic The status of the upgrade appears as Successful. Note: If you upgraded from NSX 4.1.x or earlier, new system-generated transport zones appear in addition to the transport zones originally configured in your source deployment. • For clusters prepared for networking and security, new security transport zones are created. The system generates these security transport zones by default. • For clusters prepared fo

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 7. (For upgrades from NSX 4.1.x or earlier) Create a certificate for the NSX cluster VIP, either self-signed or signed by a third-party Certificate Authority. The certificate must include the Subject Alternative Name and Subject Alternative IP for all NSX Manager nodes in the cluster. This step ensures a successful repository sync for any NSX Manager node that is redeployed in the future. For more information, See Broadcom knowledge base article 397340. Transitioning from vSphere Lifecycle Manager Baselines to vSphere Lifecycle Manager Images vSphere Lifecycle Manager baselines are not supported with VMware Cloud Foundation 9.0 or later. Before you can upgrade to ESX 9.0 or later, you must transition any clusters that use vSphere Lifecycle Manager baselines to use vSphere Lifecycle Manager images. You can transition to vSphere Lifecycle Manager images by using on of the following methods. • A PowerShell script – Menu-driven interface – Command-line interface (CLI) • The SDDC Manager API After you upgrade SDDC Manager to version 9.0 or later, you must transition all clusters in the management domain and in all workload domains. If the transition is not possible or you have vSphere S

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Category Prerequisite vSphere Lifecycle Manager image To use the PowerShell script or SDDC Manager API, you must have at least one vLCM image available. While these images are originally authored in vCenter, the required format has changed: • Version 8.0.3 and earlier: Images must be created a zero-host, vLCM-managed cluster. • Version 9.0 and later: Images should be authored using the vCenter Image Library. For information about creating a temporary cluster with a vLCM image, see Creating and Managing vSphere Lifecycle Manager Clusters. Choose the documentation section corresponding to the version in the following table. Note: vCenter Version Documentation Section 8.0 Update 3 Create a Cluster That Uses a Single Image by Importing an Image from a Host 8.0 through 8.0 Update 2 Create a Cluster That Uses a Single Image by Composing a New Image For information about creating an Image Library vLCM image in vCenter 9, see Create an Image in Image Library Manually. Note: The ESX version in the image must match the current version of the ESX hosts in the cluster you are transitioning. Note: Use a descriptive name for the images that you create. Important Considerations • You cannot combine the process of transitioning to vSphere Lifecycle manager images with the process of upgrading ESX. • vSphere clusters that use vSphere Lifecycle Manager baselines must be transitioned to use images before they can be upgraded to ESX 9.0 or later. • During the transition process, you cannot perform any other operations on the cluster or t

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: environment, the created image must be uniform across all hosts, with matching VIBs. Upon completion, the image is stored in the vCenter depot. Transitioning vSphere Lifecycle Manager Baseline Clusters to vSphere Lifecycle Manager Image Clusters Using PowerShell You can use the PowerShell script through a menu-driven interface or a command-line interface (CLI). The menu-driven interface provides a guided transition process and is best suited for smaller environments. The command-line interface is useful for larger environments and can be used to operate against a single cluster at a time or, at scale, using JSON files. Attention: After you start a task with PowerShell, entering Ctrl-C or exiting PowerShell will not halt many tasks (for example, image import, compliance checks, or transition tasks). Those tasks continue to run in the background in SDDC Manager or vCenter. Notice: If the script execution system does not trust the certificate of the SDDC Manager, execute the following lines before running the script: Set-PowerCLIConfiguration -InvalidCertificateAction Ignore -Scope User $Env:SkipCertificateCheck="enabled" Logging The script records all actions to time-stamped files in

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: import esx-803-m01 followed by esx-803-w01, SDDC manager rejects esx-803-w01. This validation step occurs during the final stage—after the full image has been uploaded to SDDC Manager. 1. Launch the script. .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 2. Connect to SDDC Manager and select a vCenter that manages the vSphere clusters you want to transition. a) From the main menu of the script, select Connect to SDDC Manager and select vCenter . If a credentials file is not found on disk, you are prompted to provide the SDDC Manager FQDN and administrative credentials. If an SDDC credential file is present, the script uses those credentials to connect to the SDDC Manager referenced in the file. b) Select the vCenter(s) to which you want to connect. You can connect to all vCenters, a single vCenter, or a comma-delimited list of multiple vCenters. VMware by Broadcom 2250

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 3. Import a vSphere Lifecycle Manager image into SDDC Manager from any connected vCenter. Manually importing a vLCM image into SDDC Manager is only required if your clusters or standalone hosts do not support image seeding. a) From the main menu of the script, Import vLCM images from vCenter(s) into SDDC Manager. b) Select the ID of the image to import and wait for the task to complete. If the import task fails with the error message An image named <image-name> already exists in the SDDC Manager, rename the source image in vCenter or delete the conflicting image from SDDC Manager before VMware by Broadcom 2251

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: retrying. To delete the conflicting image in SDDC Manager, return to the main menu and select (Optional) Delete SDDC Manager Image . c) Repeat these steps for all the images you want to import. 4. Check vSphere Lifecycle Manager baseline clusters or standalone hosts for compliance with a vSphere Lifecycle Manager image. Be mindful of the executing logic: The menu-driven interface always processes clusters sequentially. Each compliance check of a cluster must finish before the next one begins. Parallel checks are only possible using the command-line interface with the -Parallel option. Standalone hosts, however, are processed in parallel, so long as the following conditions are met: • Each batch does not exceed 25 standalone hosts • Each batch contains identical images or uses image seeding • Each batch uses the same workload domain vCenter. Attention: The compliance check mis-identifies certain non-user managed VIBs and components as non-compliant and in need of removal. The underlying files are not missing, but rather the metadata describing the entities is not yet installed. The metadata installation occurs automatically post-vLCM image transition. If the compliance check identif

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Solution Type VIB/Component Name vSphere Replication VIB vmware-hbr-agent For more information, see Broadcom knowledge base article 311882. a) From the main menu of the script, select Check existing cluster(s)/standalone host(s)' vLCM image compliance. b) Enter a comma-delimited list of the IDs of the vSphere clusters and/or standalone hosts that you want to scan for compliance. c) When image seeding is supported across all selected clusters, you have the following two options. • Yes: Automatically generate an image based on the current cluster state • No: Manually select a vLCM image from the SDDC Manager inventory. The script returns a high-level status and summary and saves a JSON file with the full findings for each vSphere cluster or standalone host. d) Review the summary report to determine if a vSphere cluster or standalone host is ready to be transitioned. Resolve any issues for vSphere clusters with a status of INCOMPATIBLE or UNKNOWN. Status Description NON_COMPLIANT The vSphere cluster or standalone host is not currently compliant with the selected vSphere Lifecycle Manager image, but is ready to be transitioned. INCOMPATIBLE The vSphere cluster or standalone host cannot

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 5. Transition NON_COMPLIANT (ready to transition) vSphere clusters or standalone hosts to vSphere Lifecycle Manager images. CAUTION: After you start a compliance or transition task, it cannot be paused or stopped. The task continues to run on the SDDC manager even if the Powershell window is closed. a) From the main menu, Transition vLCM baseline (VUM) cluster/standalone host to vLCM image management. b) Enter a comma-delimited list of the IDs of the vSphere clusters or standalone hosts that you want to transition to vSphere Lifecycle Manager images. c) Review the compliance findings from those vSphere clusters and/or standalone hosts, and if you wish to proceed, enter Y to begin the workflow. d) After you select your clusters and/or standalone hosts, you might choose to temporarily override remediation policies for the transition operation. See Configuring Remediation Settings for further details. Example of a cluster transition with an override remediation policy VMware by Broadcom 2258

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Return to the main menu . • Select Option 6, (Optional) Retry incomplete transition tasks. . • Wait some time then select Option 9,(Optional) Show cluster transition status. . • If the process succeeds, no further action is required, if it fails, contact Broadcom Support. 6. Disconnect from SDDC Manager and vCenter. Close the PowerShell window or enter Q to quit. Transition to vSphere Lifecycle Manager Images Using the PowerShell Script Command-Line Interface The PowerShell script command-line interface (CLI) is useful for larger environments. The CLI can be used to transition a single resource command-line arguments or multiple resources using JSON input files. See PowerShell Script CLI Options for Transitioning to vSphere Lifecycle Manager Images for information about all the CLI options. Attention: For bulk operations using a JSON file, use a JSON validator to ensure that your file is properly formed before using it as an input. Attention: Appending -Silence to any command suppresses screen output and any progress indicators. Pertinent data is still sent to the log file and task operation status might still be queried through relevant check commands. This process can be useful

Command Description .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ShowBaselineRe- sources Displays a list of baseline resources (clusters and standalone hosts). .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ShowBaselineRe- sources -JsonOutput path_to_file Displays a list of baseline-managed resources (clusters and standalone hosts) and then saves the output to a file. 4. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Command Description .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ShowImagesInV- center -JsonOutput path_to_file -Silence Only saves vCenter image output to a file, and does not display output to the screen. 5. Import vSphere Lifecycle Manager images into SDDC Manager from connected vCenter instances. Command Description .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ImportImages- FromVcenter -VcenterImageName image_name-Vcenter- Name vCenter_FQDN Import a single image from vCenter and display progress on screen. .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ImportImages- FromVcenter -VcenterImageName image_name -Vcenter- Name vCenterFQDN -Silence Import a single image from vCenter and do not display progress on screen. \VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ImportImages- FromVcenter -JsonInput Path_To_File -VcenterName v- Center_FQDN Import multiple images from vCenter from a JSON input file serially and display progress on screen. Example JSON file for bulk import: [ { "VcenterImageName": "image_name", "VcenterName": "vCenter_FQDN" }, { "VcenterImageName": "another_image_name", "VcenterName": "vCenter_FQDN" } ] VMware by Broadcom 2262

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Command Description .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ImportImages- FromVcenter -JsonInput path_to_file -VcenterName v- Center_FQDN -Parallel Import multiple images from vCenter from a JSON input file in parallel. Note: If the script is called without the flag -Parallel , it attempts to delete empty vSphere clusters after a successful import. The script does not delete vSphere clusters with hosts or images from the vCenter image catalog. To check the task status, run the following command: .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -CheckTaskStatus -TaskType SddcManagerImageUpload Note: If the import task fails with the error message An image named <image-name> already exists in the SDDC Manager, you can rename the source image in vCenter or delete the conflicting image from SDDC Manager before retrying. To delete the conflicting image in SDDC Manager, run the following command: .\VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -DeleteImageFromSddcManager -SddcManagerImageName image_name 6. Review the images available in SDDC Manager. Command Description ./VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -ShowImagesInSd- dcManager Displays a list of all vSphere Lifecycle Manager images in SDDC Manager. The underlying files are not missing, but rather the metadata describing the entities is not yet installed. The metadata installation occurs automatically post-vLCM image transition. If the compliance check identifies the removal of these entities as the exclusive trigger for maintenance mode, the hosts will not transition into maintenance mode during the operation, The transition instead can be performed live. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Command Description ./VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 -TransitionRe- sources -JsonInput path_to_file -Parallel Transitions multiple vSphere clusters and/or standalone hosts in parallel to use a vSphere Lifecycle Manager image and does not display progress. You can run compliance checks in parallel, but there is a concurrent task limit of 25 compliance and transition tasks. If you exceed this limit, the task is rejected, rather than queued. Important: The system transitions the cluster or standalone hosts using the last image that you checked against. If you run the compliance check multiple times against multiple images, run ./VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 - ShowBaselineResources to review the final association before proceeding. Retrieve the names and IDs of vSphere clusters that are managed using vSphere Lifecycle Manager baselines. a) Log in to the SDDC Manager UI. b) Click Developer Center > API Explorer. c) Expand Clusters and click GET /v1/clusters. d) Enter the value false for the isImageBased parameter and click Execute. e) Click Download to save the results. You can rename the saved file using a more meaningful name. f) Review the file to identify the vSphere cluster ID associated with each vSphere cluster name. Example: VMware by Broadcom 2271

Click Refresh to see the current list of images or vSphere clusters. e) Select the image (vCenter 9.0 and later) or vSphere cluster (vCenter 8.0.3 and below). For vCenter 8.0.3 and below, enter a name for your image. f) Click Import. You can monitor the status in the Tasks panel. g) Repeat the process for each unique image you want to import. 3. Check a vSphere Lifecycle Manager baseline-managed vSphere cluster for compliance with a vSphere Lifecycle Manager image. You can run a vSphere cluster compliance check multiple times, but only the latest check (and associated image) is used for transitioning the vSphere cluster. Attention: The compliance check mis-identifies certain non-user managed VIBs and components as non-compliant and in need of removal. INCOMPATIBLE The vSphere cluster cannot be transitioned to the selected vSphere Lifecycle Manager image without taking additional steps. Review the summary and JSON file and resolve any issues that are blocking the transition. Contact Broadcom Support if you are unable to resolve the issue(s). UNKNOWN Compatibility could not be determined. Make sure that all the hosts in the vSphere cluster are available. Contact Broadcom Support if you are unable to resolve the issue. COMPLIANT All hosts in the vSphere cluster confirm to the vLCM image. a) Expand Clusters and click GET /v1/clusters/{id}/image-compliance b) Enter the vSphere cluster ID for the id parameter. c) Click Execute. d) Click Download to save the results. e) Review the results using the tool of your choice. 5. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Licensing Use a VCF Operations instance and the VCF Business Services console as a VI administrator to manage and assign licenses. In This Chapter • Licensing Overview • Registering VCF Operations with the VCF Business Services console • Switch from Connected to Disconnected Mode • Switch from Disconnected to Connected Mode • Managing Licenses in the VCF Business Services Console • Add a License to VCF Operations and License Server • Assign a Primary License to a vCenter Instance • Assign an Add-on License to a vCenter Instance • What is an Override License • Reclaim Used Capacity • Updating Licenses and Viewing the License Usage File • Remove a License from a vCenter Instance • Remove a License from a License Server and the Respective VCF Operations Instance • Delete a License Server • Deactivate a VCF Operations Instance • Reactivate a VCF Operations Instance • License Usage Analytics • License Management for Cloud Services Providers and Hyperscalers • Move a Contract or a Serial Number Between Site IDs • A License Server Does Not Appear in the License Servers Table in VCF Operations Getting to Know VCF To apply this documentation, you must be acquainted with the VCF Product Over

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • You can assign a license directly to some assets other than vCenter, for example, ESX hosts, and vSAN clusters. The licenses that you assign directly to assets override the vCenter license that is assigned automatically to these assets. For more information, see What is an Override License. Overview of Core Procedures and Concepts in 9.1 • To license your environment, you purchase a term-based subscription for a product with a specified capacity. • A license is an object that entitles you to use the products that you purchased subscriptions for. A license can span multiple subscriptions or, when you split the default license, a license can be a portion of a subscription. • VMware Cloud Foundation and vSphere Foundation include different sets of product components. You purchase a subscription for a specific product. With the provided license, you license all product components by using a registered VCF Operations instance. • The initial license that is automatically created in VCF Business Services console after you purchase a subscription is called a default license. The default license is a pool of capacity from all active subscriptions for the same product in the same Site ID.

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • License expiration applies if the subscription expires or if usage is not reported and the license is not refreshed: – Notifications are displayed before the license expiration. – After the license expires, you have 90 days to update your license. License assignments are impacted during this 90 day period and notifications appear in most components, including VCF Operations and vCenter. – 90 days after the expiration, your environment is impacted in several ways, including: • Management operations of various components might be prevented; • ESX hosts disconnect from vCenter, most host management operations are prevented; • You cannot start workloads. Note: Existing workloads are not proactively stopped. – You can recover expired environments by downloading an updated license file. • With your subscription for a product, you receive both a version 9+ default license, and license keys for version 8.x of the product. You can decide which version of the product to deploy and license. Your total used capacity must not exceed your purchased capacity. • You can check your license consumption analytics in your VCF Operations instance, and the VCF Business Services console. For more infor

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Remove (from VCF Operations and license server) Performed in theVCF Business Services console. When you remove a license from a license server, the license is no longer available to assign in the VCF Operations instance, and you can add the license to a different license server and, respectively, a different VCF Operations instance. For more information, see Remove a License from a License Server and the Respective VCF Operations Instance. Delete Performed in the VCF Business Services console. After you delete a license, it is no longer available and the allocated capacity from this license is automatically merged into the capacity of the corresponding default license. For more information, see Delete a License. Assign Performed in VCF Operations. The action of entitling a vCenter instance the cores or TiBs on assets within the vCenter instance, including hosts or vSAN clusters, and any connected component, for example NSX. For more information, see Assign a Primary License to a vCenter Instance or Assign an Add-on License to a vCenter Instance. You can also assign a license directly to assets which consume license capacity such as an ESX host, a vSAN cluster, a VMware Private AI F

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: vCenter license The license that you assign to a vCenter instance. You can assign both primary and add-on licenses to a vCenter instance. When you assign a license to a vCenter instance, it is automatically assigned to all assets connected to that vCenter instance that correspond to the type of license. For example, a VCF license is assigned to the ESX hosts, and a vSAN license is assigned to the vSAN clusters. If override licenses are assigned to the assets, the vCenter license is not assigned to them. Evaluation mode After you install a product or component, it operates in evaluation mode for up to 90 days. During that period, you must license your environment. Evaluation mode ends when a license is assigned or at the end of the 90-day period, whichever comes first. License category The license categories are default, split, and shared. License type The types of licenses are primary and add-on. License server A license server is added to a VCF Operations instance to store the licenses you want to assign from this VCF Operations instance. You must add at least one license server to each VCF Operations instance that you use for license management. License Unit The unit of measure f

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Pooling Combining the total capacity of subscriptions for the same product in one license. Registration file A file used to identify a VCF Operations instance, and list all license servers added to this VCF Operations instance. The name format of the registration file is: registration- <vcfOpsName><timestamp>.registration Verification file A file that verifies the integrity of a license server. You download a verification file from the VCF Business Services console and import it in VCF Operations. The name format of a verification file is: verification- <vcfOpsName><timestamp>.verification Confirmation file A file that completes the verification of the integrity of a license server. You download a confirmation file from VCF Operations and import it in the VCF Business Services console. The name format of a confirmation file is: confirmation-<vcfOpsName><timestamp>.confirmation License file The license file contains the licenses added to a VCF Operations instance from the VCF Business Services console. The name format of the license file is: license- <vcfOpsName><timestamp>.lic Licensing Model The licensing model is the same for VCF and vSphere Foundation. You assign licenses to vCe

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: What Are Primary Licenses? Primary licenses are the licenses for VCF and vSphere Foundation. They are based on a core license unit. The only assets in your environment which consume the capacity of your primary licenses are ESX hosts. To calculate the capacity you need for your environment, you need the total number of the physical CPU cores for each physical CPU on all ESX hosts in your environment. Most products have a minimum consumption rule of 16 cores per physical CPU. CPUs that have fewer than 16 cores consume 16 cores. For example, if you have 1 ESX host in your inventory, with 1 CPU, and 8 CPU cores per CPU, this ESX host consumes 16 cores of your license total capacity because it is the minimum license capacity. Number of ESX Hosts Number of CPUs per Host Cores per CPU Required License Capacity 1 1 8 16 2 2 8 64 2 2 16 64 2 2 24 96 For more information about calculating the license capacity you need for your environment, see Broadcom knowledge base article 95927. You assign primary licenses to your vCenter instances and optionally as overrides to individual hosts. When a primary license is assigned to a vCenter instance, the other components of the product are licensed au

If the 90-day evaluation period of a vCenter instance expires, all hosts added to the vCenter instance are disconnected from it. b. If the 90-day evaluation period of an ESX host expires, but the vCenter instance is licensed, only the ESX host with an expired evaluation period is disconnected from the vCenter. 3. You start workloads. Note: Existing workloads are not proactively stopped. If the evaluation period expires, you can recover the operations in your environment by assigning a valid license. 1. If the 90-day evaluation period of a VCF Operations instance has expired, you must register the instance and add a valid license to it. 2. If the 90-day evaluation period of a vCenter instance has expired, you must assign a valid license to it from VCF Operations. 3. If the 90-day evaluation period of an ESX host has expired, you must add the host to a vCenter instance that has a valid license with enough available capacity assigned to it. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: You can add ESXi 8.x hosts that are licensed with license keys to your vCenter instances of version 9.1. Those ESXi hosts must be licensed with a pre-version 9 license key, and they also consume license capacity from your version 9+ license from your VCF or vSphere Foundation subscription. The used license capacity displayed in VCF Operations for this vCenter instance of version 9.1 includes the license capacity used by the licensed ESX hosts of versions 9.0, 9.1, and 8.x that are added to this vCenter instance. Used capacity for the license displayed in the VCF Operations instance and the VCF Business Services console also reflects this. Version 8.x licenses cannot be assigned as override licenses. If you want to upgrade an ESX host of version 9.0 to version 9.1, the host should either have remaining evaluation period, or it must be added to a vCenter instance of version 9.1 that is connected to a license server. Note: In a VMware Cloud Foundation Instance, the management domain of version 9.1 can manage workload domains of version 5.x, 9.0, and 9.1. License usage data is collected for version 9+ licenses, and for licenses of earlier versions connected to VCF Operations. You can v

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: The vSAN clusters reflect the total raw storage capacity available. For more information about calculating the license capacity that you need for your environment, see Broadcom knowledge base article 95927. If you have a bare metal host as a vSAN witness, perform the following: 1. Log in to the witness host. 2. Run the following command: esxcli vsan witness license set --enable true 3. Reboot the witness host. vSAN License Capacity in VMware Cloud Foundation If you have a vSAN cluster with 3 ESX hosts, 1 CPU per host, and each host has 6 physical cores per CPU, you must purchase the subscription capacity of 16 cores per CPU as it is the required minimum license capacity. For each VCF cores that you purchase, you receive 1 TiB of vSAN capacity. With a total of 48 (3 * 1 * 16) physical cores per VCF cluster, you receive 48 TiB vSAN capacity. Similarly, with a total of 144 (3 * 2 * 24) physical cores per VCF cluster, you receive 144 TiB vSAN capacity. Number of ESX hosts (in vSAN cluster) CPUs per ESX Host Cores per CPU Number of Core License Entitled vSAN Capacity (TIB) 3 1 6 (minimum 16 cores required) 48 48 3 2 24 144 144 To expand the capacity beyond the total entitled tebibytes,

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: The 0.25 TiB of vSAN capacity per licensed core pools with any vSAN capacity from other add-ons or VCF purchases. You can use this capacity to license the vSAN clusters in their vCenter instances, regardless of whether the capacity is more or less than 0.25 TiB per core. License Server Overview A license server is added to a VCF Operations instance to store the licenses you want to assign from this VCF Operations instance. You must add at least one license server to each VCF Operations instance that you use for license management. Licenses for VCF infrastructure and management components are stored on a dedicated license server appliance. The license server is a protected repository within your environment to keep your licensing data. The license server is automatically deployed during new installations or upgrades. The lifecycle management of the license server is performed by VCF Operations. If you need more license servers, you can manually download and install them. License Server Models • Single License Server Model: This model provides a single, unified repository for the entire fleet. It is suitable for deployments where reduced operational overhead is prioritized. • Multi L

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Table 958: Ports and protocols used for licensing-related operations Source Destination Port Protocol Description vCenter License server HTTPS 443 TCP When a license is assigned to a vCenter instance, or when a host is added to or removed from a licensed vCenter instance, the vCenter instance connects to the license server. License server VCF Operations HTTPS 443 TCP The license server regularly connects to VCF Operations to check for updates, provide license usage data, and retrieve updated license files. License server DNS servers 53 UDP The license server connects to the specified DNS servers for name resolution. VCF Operations vCenter HTTPS 443 TCP VCF Operations connects to vCenter instances to coordinate the assignment of available licenses to the vCenter instances. No other components require a network connection to the license server. If your VCF Operations instance is registered in disconnected mode, you must manually submit aggregated license usage data. If your VCF Operations instance is registered in connected mode, it periodically communicates aggregated license usage data to Broadcom. Table 959: Ports and protocols for communication with Broadcom License servers perio

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: VCF Operations handles all other licensing-related communication. For example, in connected mode, VCF Operations periodically communicates aggregated license usage data to Broadcom. In disconnected mode, you must manually submit that data. For more information, see Updating Licenses and Viewing the License Usage File. The license server uses the time set on the host which contains the license server virtual machine. To avoid license assignment failure, ensure that the time on the ESX host which contains the license server virtual machine is synchronized with the time set on the other VCF components. The license server does not support SSH connections, and you cannot enable SSH on the license server. Root or other user accounts are not present on the license server. License Server Downtime Impact If the license server is unavailable, you cannot assign licenses, generate license usage reports, or import license files. The license server does not need to be always online. There is no immediate workload or management operations impact if the license server becomes unreachable by VCF Operations or vCenter instances. To minimize potential downtime, ensure that the cluster which contains

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Registering VCF Operations with the VCF Business Services console You access and manage the licenses for your environment by using VCF Operations, and the VCF Business Services console. You must register the VCF Operations instance and a license server with the VCF Business Services console. You must install a VCF Operations instance version 9.1 to manage licenses for VCF components of version 9.1. If you want to access and manage your 9.1 licenses, and you already have an earlier version of VCF Operations installed, you must upgrade the instance to version 9.1. A license server is automatically deployed as part of the installation and upgrade of VCF. Each license server contains licenses for a specific VCF Operations instance. You can add licenses for different products in the same license server, and you can add as many licenses to a license server as you need. You can add licenses to the license server at any time after the initial registration process. For more information, see Add a License to VCF Operations and License Server. You need at least one license server and a VCF Operations instance to license your environment. If you need another license server, you can download an

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: If you do not want to include the FQDN of the VCF Operations instance or the hostname of the license server in the registration details, you can use the following API before you start the registration process: PUT {VCF_Ops_URI}/suite-api/api/deployment/config/globalsettings/GENERATE_DEFAULT_LICENSE_MANAG- ER_ASSET_NAME/false How to view the detailed contents of your registration file If your VCF Operations instance is in connected mode, the registration details are sent automatically by using a combination of query string parameters and API parameters. If your VCF Operations instance is in disconnected mode, you must manually download the registration information as a JSON Web Signed (JWS) file. The information provided to Broadcom for registration is equivalent between connected and disconnected mode, but sent in different formats. Note: You can view the registration file contents only in disconnected mode. 1. Before you complete the registration process, save a copy of your registration file. 2. Use a text editor to open the file, and copy the content. 3. Paste the content of the registration file into a JWT decoding tool. After the content is decoded, you can view the collected

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. Log in to VCF Operations. 2. From the navigation bar at the top, click Manage and in the left navigation pane, click Licensing > Licenses & Registration. 3. In the Registration and License Server Status card, click Manage > Registration Details. The VCF Operations Registration page appears. 4. In the Registration Details tab, next to Reporting Mode, click Switch to Disconnected. 5. In the Switch Reporting Mode dialog box, click Switch to Disconnected. Your VCF Operations instance operates in disconnected mode. You must manually report license usage, and then upload license files in VCF Operations. For more information about reporting license usage and updating your licenses, see Report License Usage and Update Licenses in Disconnected Mode. Switch from Disconnected to Connected Mode You can switch from disconnected to connected mode at any time. In connected mode, license usage reports are submitted automatically, and the update of licenses is easier. To switch from disconnected to connected mode, verify that your environment is connected to the Internet. If your VCF Operations instance operates in disconnected mode for more than 180 days, switching from disconnected to connecte

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Split Default Licenses You can split default licenses into as many licenses as you need. You can allocate part of the default license capacity to each of the new licenses that you create as a result of the split. When you create a split license, the capacity you add to it is extracted from the available capacity in the default license. If the available capacity in the default license is less than the capacity that you want to add to the new split license, you can reduce the capacity of other split licenses. The capacity that you deduct from split licenses is added back as available capacity in the default license. 1. In the VCF Business Services console, navigate to Licensing > Licenses. 2. Click the Version 9+ tab. 3. Click Manage Licenses (Split/Merge). The Manage Licenses wizard appears. 4. From the drop-down menu, select a product for which you want to split the default license. The default license and all previous split licenses for this product appears. 5. Click Create. A new line for a split license appears in the table. 6. In the Name column, enter a name for the license. 7. In the New Capacitycolumn, enter the amount of capacity you want to allocate to the new license. You

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: You cannot reduce the capacity of a license below the quantity of the license that is currently used. If you try to reduce the capacity of an assigned license below the used capacity, the operation fails. For example, if you have a license with capacity of 250 cores, and it is assigned to a vCenter instance, which has 4 ESX hosts added to it, each using 50 cores of license capacity, you cannot reduce the capacity of the license to less than 200 cores. Note: If at a certain point the used capacity of a license is greater than the capacity allocated to the license, you must allocate more capacity to that license. Until you add more capacity, you cannot assign the over used license to additional vCenter instances. If a vCenter instance is licensed by a license that is over used, you can only add new ESX hosts if they are in evaluation mode. 1. In the VCF Business Services console, navigate to Licensing > Licenses. 2. Click the vertical ellipsis next to the split license, and select Change Capacity. 3. In the New Capacitycolumn, enter the new amount of allocated capacity. For example, if your license has 150 cores of allocated capacity, and you want to add 50 cores, you add 200 in the

You cannot delete a split license if some of its capacity is assigned to a vCenter instance or another asset. • Verify that the license is not assigned to a vCenter instance or another asset. • Verify that the license is not added to a license server.
