title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: If you try to add a host to a licensed vCenter instance, and the cores of the host are more than the available capacity left in the license assigned to the vCenter instance, the host is added in evaluation mode. If the host has exceeded the 90-day evaluation period, you cannot add it to the vCenter instance unless you increase the available capacity of the license. Note: If the host is in evaluation mode, it is added to the vCenter instance even if the license capacity is not sufficient. During the evaluation period of the host, you must add more capacity to the license to license the host. Alternatively, you can assign an override license to the host. If the host is not licensed when the evaluation period expires, it is disconnected from the vCenter instance. The virtual machines running on the disconnected host remain intact. When there is enough available capacity in the license assigned to the vCenter instance, all disconnected hosts reconnect to the vCenter system. For example, you add a license with 1000 cores allocated capacity to the VCF Operations instance. You then add 4 vCenter instances to be managed by this VCF Operations instance. The aggregate license consumption of

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Verify that you have the Manage Licenses permission assigned. • Verify that your VCF Operations instance is registered with the VCF Business Services console. • Verify that add-on licenses are added to the VCF Operations instance. • Verify that the vCenter instance to which you want to assign a license is integrated with the VCF Operations instance. If the vCenter instance is part of a VCF domain, verify that the VCF Instance is integrated with the VCF Operations instance. For more information about adding vCenter instances and VCF Instances to VCF Operations, see Configuring a vCenter Account in VCF Operations, or Configuring a Standalone VCF Account in VCF Operations. You cannot assign a license to a vCenter instance if that instance requires more license capacity than the available license capacity of the license you want to assign. To be able to use an add-on license, you must first add a primary license to your VCF Operations instance, and assign it to the vCenter instances. With your VCF subscription, in addition to your VMware Cloud Foundation (cores) license you receive a vSAN (TiBs) license. Starting November 3, 2025, depending on your VCF subscription, you might also re

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: You cannot assign override licenses to ESX hosts of versions earlier than 9.0. When you remove a host from the vCenter instance, the license capacity it uses is added back to the license assigned to the host. If the host is licensed with an override license, the license capacity is added back to the override license. If the host is licensed with a vCenter license, the license capacity is added back to the vCenter license. If the vCenter license expires, you can assign an override license to an ESX host but you must also assign a valid license to the vCenter instance. If you do not assign a valid license to the vCenter instance, you cannot use the management capabilities of the vCenter instance and you can only manage the host as a standalone host. Evaluation Mode and Override Licenses When you add an ESX host of version 9.0 or later to a vCenter instance of version 9.1, the vCenter license is assigned to the host automatically if the license capacity is sufficient to license the host. If the license capacity is insufficient, the host is added in evaluation mode. The evaluation period is 90 days, during that time you can either add more capacity to the vCenter license, or assign an

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: You cannot assign override licenses to ESX hosts of versions earlier than 9.0. The override and vCenter license must meet compatibility criteria. Compatibility is checked at the time when you assign a license. If the compatibility check fails, the license assignment is unsuccessful. For more information about override licenses, see What is an Override License. 1. Log in to VCF Operations. 2. From the navigation bar at the top, click Manage and in the left navigation pane, click Licensing > Licenses & Registration. 3. Select the Version 9+ tab. 4. From the vCenter Systems table, click the vCenter instance to which the ESX host is added. The table contains only vCenter instances of version 9+ managed by the current VCF Operations instance. If a vCenter instance is added to the VCF Operations instance for monitoring only, it does not appear in this table. 5. In the page with the vCenter license details, navigate to Assets, and click ESX Hosts. 6. Select the check box next to the host to which you want to assign an override license, and click Assign Override License. 7. In the Assign Override License page, select the license that you want to assign to the host, and click Assign. The li

Assign an override license to an ESX host. a) Under Assets, click ESX Hosts. b) Select the check box next to the hosts to which you want to assign an override license, and click Switch to vCenter License. A dialog box appears. c) Click Replace. The ESX hosts are licensed with the license assigned to the vCenter instance. The License Source column shows vCenter. 8. Assign an override license to a vSAN cluster. a) Under Assets, click vSAN Clusters. b) Select the check box next to the vSAN clusters to which you want to assign an override license, and click Switch to vCenter License. A dialog box appears. c) Click Replace. The vSAN clusters are licensed with the license assigned to the vCenter instance. The License Source column shows vCenter. Reclaim Used Capacity When you delete an asset, your license capacity is automatically reclaimed and available to assign to a new asset. If a connectivity issue between the license server and the vCenter instance occurs, the license capacity might not be automatically added back to the license. If this happens, you can manually reclaim the used capacity. • Verify that the ass

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. Log in to VCF Operations. 2. From the navigation bar at the top, click Manage and in the left navigation pane, click Licensing > Licenses & Registration. 3. In the Registration and License Server Status card, click Manage > License Servers. The VCF Operations Registration page appears. 4. In the License Servers tab, click the license server which contains the license that is assigned to the removed asset. 5. Under Licensed Assets, select the asset for which you want to reclaim the capacity, and click Reclaim Used Capacity. A dialog box appears. 6. Click Reclaim Used Capacity. The license capacity used by the removed asset is added back to the license as available capacity. Updating Licenses and Viewing the License Usage File You must update your licenses in VCF Operations to verify that a usage file has been submitted, or when you make a license-related change such as subscription capacity changes, renewals, or splitting licenses and changing their capacity. When you update licenses, you do not need to reassign licenses to the assets. If you do not make any license-related changes in the VCF Business Services console, you must still update your licenses at least once every 6 mon

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: License Usage File Content Property Applies to version Description usage_report_generated_time 9.0 and later The date and time when the usage report is generated. acknowledged_claims 9.1 and later This property is for reserved licenses. If you have no reserved licenses, the property is NULL. additional_data 9.1 and later Additional records include the count of objects instead of just aggregate core and TiB counts. For more information, see the table Table 1. usage_report_id 9.0 and later The unique ID of this usage report. asset_id 9.0 and later The unique ID of the VCF Operations instance where data is collected. asset_status 9.0 and later Active. xr2 9.0 and later A unique opaque fingerprint of the VCF Operations instance where data is collected; used in conjunction with the asset_id to link the usage data to the registered VCF Operations instance. No identifiable environment details can be derived from the fingerprint. jws_child_asset_usages 9.1 and later For each license server that is available for this VCF Operations instance, a unique usage report is included that reflects changes to the license usage. This content is encoded. You can decode it by using any JWT token decoder

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Property Applies to version Description recycled_events.RecycleStats.assets_recycled 9.1 and later The count of licensed assets for which license capacity was reclaimed and were later relicensed. recycled_events.RecycleStats.max_recycle_freque ncy 9.1 and later The highest frequency in which a licensed asset received a new license after having the license reclaimed. recycled_events.avg_recycle_frequency 9.1 and later The average frequency in which licensed assets received a new license after having it removed. Additional records in the usage file include the count of objects instead of only aggregate core and TiB counts. The additional_data object from the license usage file contains several properties. text: Deactivate a VCF Operations Instance When you deactivate a VCF Operations instance from the VCF Business Services console, you can no longer add any licenses to that instance, and you cannot perform any license management operations. • Verify that there are no licensed vCenter or VCF Instances managed by this VCF Operations instance. • Delete all license servers from the VCF Operations instance. For more information, see Delete a License Server. • If the VCF Operations instance is in disconnected mode, report license usage. For more information, see Report License Usage and Update Licenses in Disconnected Mode. Note: If you no longer have access to the VCF Operations instance, you can deactivate the instance without following the prerequisites listed above. Once you deactivate a VCF Operations instance, if you want to reactivate it, the same licenses that are added to it before the deactivation might not be available. Note: When you deactivate a VCF Operations instance, all licenses servers that are added to this instance are automatically deleted. 1. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: View Product License Usage in the VCF Business Services console In the VCF Business Services console, you can view aggregated data for your complete license usage for all your products and licenses in the unit of measure for the specific license. You can use the analytics data to monitor license usage and plan your license management. You can also export and view the usage analytics data for product aggregate usage, version 9+ license usage, and pre- version 9 license usage. The export file is available in CSV and JSON formats. 1. Log in to the VCF Business Services console (vcf.broadcom.com). 2. Navigate to Licensing > Usage Analytics. 3. In the Usage by Product pane, click the product for which you want to view detailed license usage. The Usage Details page for the selected product appears. 4. If you want to view usage analytics for a specific license, use the Select License drop-down menu. 5. In the Usage History pane, you can view maximum license usage for a specific aggregation period. Use the filters to view maximum license usage in monthly or daily periods. View License Usage Per Product in VCF Operations In your VCF Operations instance, you can view aggregated data for your

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: • Verify that you have an active commit contract with Broadcom. • Verify that you have registered a license server and have installed VCF Operations 9.1 and vCenter 9.1. The vCenter should be associated with VCF Operations. Overview • To license your environment, you must have entitlements to the VCF products. • Once a commit contract is signed and available in the Broadcom Support Portal, you must create licenses against the contract serial number and the product in the contract. • Depending on the commit contract, you have a different entitlement for the maximum license capacity. If the total allocated capacity in the created licenses reaches the maximum capacity, you can no longer create new licenses. • If you both meet certain requirements, end customers can share licenses for portability with you. For more information, see License Sharing. • To license your environment, you must have an installed VCF Operations instance and a license server, register them in the VCF Business Services console, and add licenses with available capacity to a license server. You can register VCF Operations to the VCF Business Services console either in connected or in disconnected mode. Connected r

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 2. Register your VCF Operations instance with the VCF Business Services console by using one of the two modes. – Disconnected mode - no Internet connection is required. You use an activation code from the VCF Business Services console to register your VCF Operations instance and the license servers. 3. title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Restart a VCF Operations Registration If you register the VCF Operations in the wrong site or tenant, you can restart the VCF Operations registration. Before you restart the VCF Operations registration, you must remove any licenses added to the VCF Operations instance, delete the VCF Operations instance, and then re-register in the correct tenant. For more information, see Delete a License. 1. Log in to VCF Operations. 2. From the navigation bar at the top, click Manage and in the left navigation pane, navigate to License Management > Licenses & Registration. 3. In the Registration and License Server Status pane, navigate to Manage > Registration details. The VCF Operations Registration page appears. 4. In the Registration Details tab, navigate to Actions > Restart Registration. Move a Contract or a Serial Number Between Site IDs Registration of VCF Operation is linked to a VCF Business Services console tenant and a Broadcom SiteID. The license allocations are linked to their contracts via serial numbers. If a contract or serial number is moved from one Broadcom SiteID to another, this impacts existing VCF Operations that are linked to the first SiteID with licenses from that SiteI

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 2. Generate a license usage file from each VCF Operations instance that is related to the serial number, and update its licenses. a) Log in to the VCF Operations instance. b) Navigate to Licensing > Registration. c) Update licenses. • If the VCF Operations instance is in connected mode, verify that your license usage file was submitted within the last 24 hours by looking at the Last Usage Report Generated date, and click Update Licenses. For more information, see Update Licenses in Connected Mode. • If the VCF Operations instance is in disconnected mode, generate a license usage file. For more information, see Report License Usage and Update Licenses in Disconnected Mode. 3. After you move your serial number, for each VCF Operations instance, create your licenses again by splitting the default license. For more information, see Managing Licenses in the VCF Business Services Console. As a best practice, allocate the same capacity to the licenses as the capacity from the prior SiteID. Consider also using a distinct license name from the prior site. 4. Deactivate the VCF Operations instance in the VCF Business Services console. For more information, see Deactivate a VCF Operations Ins

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: After you upgrade VCF Operations from version 9.0 to version 9.1, a license server is automatically deployed and must register it with the VCF Business Services console. After the upgrade, even if you revert to a snapshot of VCF Operations before the upgrade, you cannot roll back to version 9.0 in the VCF Business Services console. License-related operations such as license usage reporting might fail. 1. Optional: Transfer the licenses from the license server to a license server that is connected to VCF Operations. The licenses are automatically assigned to the assets that they were assigned to before the transfer of licenses. 2. Delete the license server from the VCF Business Service console. For more information, see Delete a License Server. 3. Delete the license server virtual machine from the vCenter instance. VMware by Broadcom 2345

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Building Cloud Infrastructure Use VCF Operations and the vSphere Client as a VI administrator to add and configure infrastructure for consumer and management workloads in the VCF private cloud after you deploy or converge to a VCF fleet and a VCF Instance with its management domain. • You can start adding workload domains to a VCF Instance and individual clusters and hosts to these workload domains. VCF Operations deploys the vCenter and NSX Manager instances for the added workload domains. You can also enable vSphere Supervisor on the default cluster of the workload domain. • You can import vCenter instances with their managed ESX hosts as workload domains so that they are included in identity and access management, certificate management, and lifecycle management operations performed centrally in VCF Operations . • You can also add NSX network services to the management domain and the workload domains of a VCF Instance by deploying NSX Edge clusters, transit gateways, and virtual private clouds (VPCs). Then you can configure workloads to use these network services. You can place VCF Operations and VCF Automation on NSX overlay or VLAN segments so that they can use the NSX load ba

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: During the commissioning process, the ESX hosts are associated with a network pool and added to the global inventory. Newly commissioned ESX hosts appear in the global inventory with a host state of UNASSIGNED. When an ESX host is added to a workload domain, an IP address from the network pool's IP range is assigned to it. See VMware Configuration Maximums for information about ESX host maximums. Note: VMware Cloud Foundation does not support stateless ESX hosts. Network Pool Management When you create a workload domain or add an ESX host or vSphere cluster to an existing VCF domain, you only need to enter an IP address manually for the vmk0 interface. Network pools automatically assign static IP addresses to vSAN, NFS, iSCSI, and vMotion VMkernel ports. Overview A network pool in VMware Cloud Foundation is a logical container that manages the IP addressing and Layer 2 network specifications (VLAN and MTU) for the VMkernel adapters on ESX hosts. Depending on the option, it includes information about subnets reserved for the vMotion and vSAN, vSAN Storage Client, NFS, or iSCSI networks that are required for adding a host to the global inventory. Table 964: Information Required for a

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: When you create a workload domain with multiple vSphere clusters, those vSphere clusters can share a network pool or each cluster can have its own network pool. Dual-Stack Configuration In VCF 9.1, network pools are expanded to support IPv6-only configuration for vmkernel adapters for storage and vMotion traffic, allowing for greater scalability and flexibility in how storage and vMotion traffic is routed. Traffic Type Supported IP Version IP Address Assignment Mode Description Storage IPv4 or IPv6 IPv6 - Static, DHCP, SLAAC IPv4 - Static Must be dedicated to a single protocol family per cluster (all hosts use IPv6 for vSAN). vMotion IPv4 or IPv6 IPv6 - Static, DHCP, SLAAC IPv4 - Static Allows offloading bulk mobility traffic to IPv6 networks. NSX Overlay (TEP) IPv4 Only Static, DHCP VCF 9.1 does not support IPv6 for Tunnel Endpoints (TEPs). Management IPv4 or Dual-Stack Static Management interfaces support static IP assignment. You cannot mix IP versions for the same traffic type within a cluster. For example, if you assign an IPv6 vSAN network pool to a cluster, all hosts in that cluster must use IPv6 for vSAN. For Dual-Stack management pools, you must ensure that your DNS server

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: View Network Pool Details You can view the network details for a network pool as well as the total number of used and available (if IPv4) IP addresses. To perform this task in the vSphere Client, you must have access to the management domain vCenter, or your VCF Instance must be configured with VCF SSO and vCenter linking. See Configuring VCF Single Sign-On and Linking vCenter instances in VCF Operations. As an alternative, you can perform this task using the SDDC Manager UI (https://<sddc-manager-fqdn>). 1. In the vSphere Client, click Global Inventory Lists > Hosts > Network Pools. 2. Click the arrow to the left of the pool name. A summary view of the network pools details is displayed. 3. Click the name of a network pool. A detailed view of the network pool details is displayed, including network types and included IP ranges. Create a Network Pool A network pool must include the vMotion network information. Depending on the type of storage you are using, you must provide network information for vSAN, NFS, and iSCSI. The subnet in a network pool cannot overlap the subnet of another pool. To perform this task in the vSphere Client, you must have access to the management domain vCe

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: 1. In the vSphere Client, click Global Inventory Lists > Hosts > Network Pools. 2. Click Create Network Pool. 3. Enter a name for the network pool. 4. Select the network type(s). • For vSAN ESA/OSA and NFS, you can include both vSAN and NFS network information in the same network pool or create separate network pools for vSAN and NFS. • For vSAN storage, select vSAN, vSAN Storage Client, and vMotion.` • For vSAN compute, select vSAN and vMotion. • For VMFS on FC, select vMotion only. • For vVols on FC, select vMotion only or vMotion and NFS. • For vVols on iSCSI, select iSCSI. • For vVols on NFS, select vMotion and NFS. 5. Provide the following network information for the selected network type(s). Option Description IP Scheme IPv4 or IPv6 VLAN ID Enter a VLAN ID between 1 and 4094. MTU Enter an MTU between 1500 and 9000. (IPv6 Only) IP Assignment Method Select between static, DHCP, or SLAAC. Default Gateway Enter the default gateway (CIDR notation). Applicable only if IPv6 static address assignment is configured. Included IP Ranges Enter an IP address range from which an IP address can be assigned to hosts that are associated with this network pool. The IP address range must be fro

title: VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1
text: Rename a Network Pool You can rename an existing network pool. To perform this task in the vSphere Client, you must have access to the management domain vCenter, or your VCF Instance must be configured with VCF SSO and vCenter linking. See Configuring VCF Single Sign-On and Linking vCenter instances in VCF Operations. As an alternative, you can perform this task using the SDDC Manager UI (https://<sddc-manager-fqdn>). 1. In the vSphere Client, click Global Inventory Lists > Hosts > Network Pools. 2. Click the vertical ellipsis (three dots) in the network pool row you want to edit and then click Rename Network Pool. 3. Enter a new name for the network pool. 4. Click Rename. Delete a Network Pool You can delete a network pool if none of the ESX hosts with an IP address from the pool belong to a workload domain. The default pool created during deployment cannot be deleted. Ensure that the ESX hosts in the network pool are not assigned to a workload domain. To verify this, view the network pool details and confirm that the Used IPs for the network pool is 0. To perform this task in the vSphere Client, you must have access to the management domain vCenter, or your VCF Instance must be c
